questions.prompt: What happens in a chemical reaction?
choices: Atoms are separated into smaller pieces but remain the same substances.
Atoms are rearranged to form different substances.
The materials are broken into smaller pieces but keep the same substances.
The substance changes shape while its molecules stay unchanged.
questions.explanation: A chemical reaction rearranges atoms from reactants into new substances called products.

questions.prompt: Which situation most clearly involves a chemical reaction?
choices: A glass cup is warmed by sunlight but remains glass.
A pencil is sharpened so the wood and graphite become smaller pieces.
A firework burns fuel and produces hot gases and coloured light.
Water is poured into a new container and takes the container's shape.
questions.explanation: Fireworks involve substances reacting to produce new substances, heat, gases, and light.

questions.prompt: Why are some chemical reactions easy to notice while others are not?
choices: Some produce obvious signs like light or gas, while others occur quietly in living systems or materials.
Explosions are easy to notice, so quieter changes should be treated as physical changes.
Colour change is the most reliable sign, so other evidence matters less.
Laboratory reactions are easier to label, while everyday reactions are less chemical.
questions.explanation: Many reactions occur without dramatic signs, including reactions that sustain life.

questions.prompt: How is chemistry involved in digestion?
choices: Food becomes useful mainly when chewing changes it into smaller pieces.
Digestion mainly moves food through the body before cells use it unchanged.
Nutrients are produced by body temperature alone rather than chemical reactions.
Chemical reactions break food into forms that cells can use.
questions.explanation: Digestion uses chemical reactions to break down food molecules into usable nutrients.

questions.prompt: Why is it incorrect to say chemistry is separate from life?
choices: Cells use chemicals mainly when they are exposed to medicine.
Living systems depend on chemical reactions to grow, repair, and use energy.
Atoms explain materials outside the body better than body processes.
Life processes are biological, so chemical explanations are usually separate.
questions.explanation: Cells and body systems depend on chemical substances and reactions.

questions.prompt: Which example connects chemistry to health?
choices: A bandage being moved from a desk to a backpack.
A rock breaking into smaller pieces during a fall.
Body cells using nutrients after food molecules are broken down.
A thermometer reading a room temperature without changing the air.
questions.explanation: Nutrient use by cells depends on chemical processes that support health.

questions.prompt: How can chemistry support medical imaging technologies?
choices: Chemical knowledge helps design materials and processes used to visualize internal body structures.
Medical imaging depends mainly on symptoms, with materials chosen after the scan.
Chemistry is useful for medicines but less connected to imaging tools.
Medical tools depend on equipment design but not on properties of substances.
questions.explanation: Medical technologies rely on chemical understanding of materials, compounds, and body processes.

questions.prompt: Which statement best explains chemistry's role in medicine?
choices: It mainly explains dramatic examples such as fireworks.
It applies to consumer products more directly than to health care.
It replaces biological knowledge when body tissues are being studied.
It helps develop tools, drugs, materials, and methods for understanding and treating the body.
questions.explanation: Chemistry contributes to many medical advances, from imaging to treatments and materials.

questions.prompt: A patient receives a scan that depends on specially designed materials and interactions with body tissues. What concept does this illustrate?
choices: Chemical reactions are useful in products but imaging is mainly mechanical.
Chemistry can contribute to medical technologies.
Medicine uses chemistry mostly after a diagnosis has already been made.
Imaging depends on equipment but the materials' behaviour is a minor detail.
questions.explanation: Medical tools often depend on chemical properties and interactions.

kp-165.title: KP4: Chemistry in personal and consumer products

questions.prompt: Why do companies use chemistry when developing personal care products?
choices: Personal care products are chosen mainly by scent rather than material properties.
Chemistry can change product properties, but testing is less important after a formula is chosen.
They design products with specific properties such as texture, stability, scent, or cleaning ability.
Consumer products work best when ingredients are familiar, regardless of their properties.
questions.explanation: Product design depends on choosing substances with useful chemical and physical properties.

questions.prompt: Which question shows good chemical thinking about a consumer product?
choices: What ingredients give the product its properties, and how should it be used safely?
Can pleasant smell be used as the main evidence that a product is low risk?
Does the package design suggest the product has been tested enough?
Can I ignore the label if the product is familiar?
questions.explanation: Chemistry helps explain both product performance and safe use.

questions.prompt: Why can a useful household product still require caution?
choices: Useful products are safer when their ingredients are described in everyday language.
A product sold in a store has been tested, so misuse risks are usually small.
Labels mainly help compare brands rather than guide safe handling.
Useful chemical properties can also create hazards if the product is misused.
questions.explanation: A product can be helpful and still need safe handling, storage, or disposal.

kp-166.title: KP5: Chemistry in transportation and pollution

questions.prompt: How has chemistry helped transportation?
choices: It has helped vehicles rely less on material properties and reactions.
It has contributed to fuels, materials, batteries, lubricants, and safety technologies.
It mainly applies to road surfaces rather than engines or vehicle materials.
It is most relevant to fuel choice and less relevant to vehicle materials.
questions.explanation: Transportation depends on chemical knowledge of fuels, materials, and reactions.

questions.prompt: What is a negative effect connected to transportation chemistry?
choices: Transportation chemistry reduces pollution concerns enough that evidence is less important.
Vehicle materials stay inside the vehicle and do not enter the environment.
Vehicle use and production can contribute to air, land, and water pollution.
Pollution is mainly a concern during fuel use, not material production or disposal.
questions.explanation: Transportation has benefits, but fuels, emissions, and materials can create pollution.

questions.prompt: Which evaluation is most balanced?
choices: Chemistry has made transportation useful and safer, but it can also create environmental problems.
Transportation chemistry should be judged by its hazards before considering benefits.
Transportation chemistry should be judged by its benefits before considering pollution.
Environmental effects can be considered after the main technology benefits are accepted.
questions.explanation: The textbook emphasizes considering both positive and negative effects.

questions.prompt: What is a synthetic material?
choices: A material made naturally but given a new name by manufacturers.
A material from organisms that is processed without changing its properties.
A substance whose designed properties make its disposal less important.
A human-made material designed with specific properties.
questions.explanation: Synthetic materials such as plastics and synthetic fibres are made by people for useful properties.

questions.prompt: Why is plastic's stability both helpful and problematic?
choices: It means plastic keeps useful shape during use but breaks down quickly in soil.
It makes plastic durable in use, but it may persist as pollution after disposal.
It makes plastic useful mainly when products need to be disposable.
It reduces the need to think about disposal if the product is reused once.
questions.explanation: Durability is useful during a product's life but can create waste and pollution challenges.

questions.prompt: Which comparison best shows a trade-off for synthetic fibres?
choices: They should be judged mostly by whether they feel similar to natural fibres.
They are processed natural fibres rather than human-made materials.
They can be strong and stretchy, but production and disposal may have environmental impacts.
They reduce environmental concerns when durability is the main design goal.
questions.explanation: Synthetic materials should be evaluated for both useful properties and environmental effects.

questions.prompt: What makes a chemical hazardous?
choices: Its properties can cause harm if it is handled, stored, used, or disposed of incorrectly.
Its name sounds technical to someone who has not used it before.
It is used in a science classroom rather than at home.
It is a pure substance rather than a mixture.
questions.explanation: Hazards depend on chemical properties and how people interact with the substance.

questions.prompt: Why should you check hazard information before using a chemical?
choices: Chemicals with similar-looking containers usually have similar risks.
Hazard information is most useful after something goes wrong.
Classroom chemicals are familiar enough that workplace-style rules matter less.
Different hazards require different precautions.
questions.explanation: Knowing the hazard helps you choose correct equipment, behaviour, storage, and disposal.

questions.prompt: Which statement is the safest assumption?
choices: A chemical is safe if it has no smell.
A familiar chemical can still be dangerous if misused.
A small bottle usually means the hazard is too small to matter.
A liquid is harmless if it looks like water.
questions.explanation: Appearance, smell, or familiarity do not prove safety.

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of WHMIS labels?
choices: They list purchasing information before chemicals are ordered.
They give hazard information mainly after the chemical has been used.
They communicate chemical hazards in workplaces and laboratories.
They mainly identify the container contents, while SDS handle hazards.
questions.explanation: WHMIS labels provide standardized hazard information.

questions.prompt: A flame symbol appears on a chemical label. What should you infer?
choices: The chemical may present a fire hazard and needs precautions around ignition sources.
The chemical can be warmed gently if the container stays closed.
The chemical is an oxidizer, so the main concern is pressure.
The chemical should be disposed of quickly before reading the SDS.
questions.explanation: The flame pictogram warns about fire-related hazards.

questions.prompt: Why are WHMIS symbols useful when planning an investigation?
choices: They summarize hazards, so the written procedure is less important.
They show the general hazard category, so one procedure can fit most chemicals.
They are mainly useful after a spill has already happened.
They help identify risks so procedures and protective equipment can be chosen.
questions.explanation: Hazard symbols guide safe planning, handling, and response.

questions.prompt: What information can an SDS provide?
choices: A short label summary without storage or first-aid details.
Chemical properties, hazards, handling, storage, disposal, and first aid.
A list of WHMIS symbols without explaining procedures.
A general description of where the chemical is commonly used.
questions.explanation: Safety Data Sheets give detailed information needed for safe chemical work.

questions.prompt: When should you consult an SDS?
choices: After disposal, to compare the instructions with what was done.
When the container label seems confusing but the task has already started.
Before planning or carrying out work with a chemical.
When WHMIS symbols are missing, because SDS and labels serve the same role.
questions.explanation: SDS information should be checked before use so risks and procedures are understood.

questions.prompt: How are WHMIS labels and SDS related?
choices: WHMIS labels give hazard warnings, while SDS provide more detailed safety information.
They both give safety information, but labels replace SDS for simple chemicals.
SDS are used mainly for buying chemicals, while labels are used during experiments.
WHMIS labels are quick warnings, so they are consulted after the SDS.
questions.explanation: Labels alert users to hazards; SDS explain safe handling and response in more detail.

questions.prompt: Which action is appropriate before starting a chemical investigation?
choices: Put on goggles once the first visible reaction begins.
Keep long hair loose so it does not touch the goggles.
Wear required eye protection and secure loose hair or clothing.
Rely on careful handling instead of protective equipment.
questions.explanation: Protective equipment and careful behaviour reduce the chance of injury.

questions.prompt: Why should food and drink stay out of a chemistry workspace?
choices: They could become contaminated or lead to accidental ingestion of chemicals.
Food packages act like WHMIS labels for nearby chemicals.
Eating is safe as long as the chemical has no smell.
Food and drink are low risk when the chemicals are familiar household products.
questions.explanation: No eating or drinking in the lab reduces exposure and contamination risks.

questions.prompt: Which behaviour best matches safe classroom chemistry?
choices: Mix chemicals after checking that the amounts are small.
Wear open shoes if the investigation does not involve heating.
Find safety equipment locations after the procedure begins.
Follow teacher instructions and keep containers away from your face.
questions.explanation: Responsible behaviour is part of chemical safety, not just wearing equipment.

kp-172.title: KP5: Emergency response in the classroom

questions.prompt: What should you know before an investigation begins?
choices: Where personal items are stored, but not where emergency equipment is located.
Where emergency exits, eyewash stations, and other safety equipment are located.
How to clean a spill before deciding whether to notify the teacher.
How to finish the data table before responding to a hazard.
questions.explanation: Knowing emergency equipment and exits helps you respond quickly if something goes wrong.

questions.prompt: If a liquid chemical splashes on skin, what is the safest immediate response in a classroom setting?
choices: Wipe it quickly and decide later whether the skin feels irritated.
Wait to see whether it hurts before interrupting the investigation.
Rinse the area with water and notify the teacher.
Cover the area and continue if the amount seems small.
questions.explanation: Water rinsing and immediate reporting are standard first responses for many classroom spills.

questions.prompt: If something gets in a student's eye, why is an eyewash station important?
choices: It allows rapid rinsing while the teacher is notified.
It provides water for cleaning glassware after the investigation.
It reduces risk enough that goggles are less important.
It should be used after checking the SDS for first-aid details.
questions.explanation: Eye exposures require immediate rinsing and adult help.

kp-173.title: KP6: Heating, fumes, and handling containers

questions.prompt: Why should a heated test tube be pointed away from people?
choices: It helps heat spread evenly through the liquid.
It lets the observer see the colour change more clearly.
It makes fumes easier to waft from the tube.
Hot liquid or vapour could suddenly escape and cause injury.
questions.explanation: Heating can cause splashing or boiling, so the open end should not face anyone.

questions.prompt: What is the safe way to smell a substance if instructed to do so?
choices: Hold the container close and breathe in briefly.
Waft fumes gently toward your nose.
Smell directly after checking that the container is small.
Warm the substance slightly so a smaller breath is needed.
questions.explanation: Wafting reduces direct exposure to concentrated fumes.

questions.prompt: Which action is unsafe when using a flame?
choices: Knowing how to light and use the burner properly.
Keeping hair tied back.
Leaving an open flame unattended.
Handling hot objects with care.
questions.explanation: Open flames require attention because they can start fires or heat materials unexpectedly.

kp-174.title: KP7: Minimizing personal and environmental risk

questions.prompt: Which plan best minimizes both personal and environmental risk?
choices: Use correct PPE, smallest practical amounts, safe storage, and approved disposal.
Use extra chemical if the larger amount makes observations easier.
Focus on personal PPE first and decide disposal during cleanup.
Use familiar containers first and check labels if a problem appears.
questions.explanation: Risk reduction includes protecting people and preventing harmful releases to the environment.

questions.prompt: Why should disposal be part of a chemical safety plan?
choices: Disposal is mainly a classroom-cleanup issue rather than an environmental issue.
Small amounts of lab chemicals can be handled like household waste if diluted.
Disposal matters more for solids than for dissolved substances.
Improper disposal can harm people, plumbing, wastewater systems, or ecosystems.
questions.explanation: Safe chemical work continues after the investigation, including storage and disposal.

questions.prompt: A painter uses solvents at work. Which risk-reduction strategy is most complete?
choices: Wear gloves and decide where to pour waste after finishing the job.
Use ventilation and PPE, avoid spills, and dispose of waste properly.
Open containers near a heat source if the work area is well ventilated.
Rely on experience with the solvent instead of checking SDS information.
questions.explanation: Personal and environmental safety require multiple controls, not just one precaution.

questions.prompt: Why is water often called a universal solvent?
choices: It dissolves most substances at the same rate and amount.
It is usually chemically pure unless people add pollutants.
Many different substances can dissolve in it.
It carries substances in rivers more easily than through soil or groundwater.
questions.explanation: Water dissolves many substances, though not all substances and not always completely.

questions.prompt: Why does pure water rarely exist in nature?
choices: Water can dissolve natural substances such as ions and minerals as it moves through the environment.
Water changes dissolved substances into harmless materials right away.
Natural water usually contains particles but not dissolved ions.
Human-made pollutants dissolve, while naturally occurring substances usually do not.
questions.explanation: Natural water often contains dissolved substances from soil, rocks, organisms, and human activity.

questions.prompt: How does water's solvent ability relate to pollution?
choices: It keeps pollution near its original source.
It dilutes pollutants enough that their effects no longer need to be checked.
It mainly affects oceans rather than rivers or groundwater.
It can carry harmful dissolved or suspended substances through water systems.
questions.explanation: Because water can carry substances, pollutants can travel into local ecosystems and drinking water sources.

questions.prompt: Which is an example of a human source of water pollution?
choices: Dissolved minerals naturally picked up from rocks.
Pharmaceuticals or personal care products poured down drains.
Minerals entering groundwater as water moves through soil.
Water vapour leaving a lake during evaporation.
questions.explanation: Human waste, industrial waste, mining waste, plastics, and pharmaceuticals can pollute water.

questions.prompt: Why can household habits affect water quality?
choices: Household products may be diluted enough that treatment is simple.
Factories are usually the main source, so household sources can be considered later.
Substances poured down toilets, sinks, or storm drains can enter water systems.
Water treatment can be assumed to remove chemicals from household products.
questions.explanation: Everyday disposal choices can add pollutants to wastewater or stormwater.

questions.prompt: Which action would best reduce water pollution at the source?
choices: Dispose of medicines and chemicals through approved collection programs.
Flush unused products to make them disappear.
Pour small amounts of solvents into a street drain during heavy rain.
Assume small amounts are too diluted to affect water quality.
questions.explanation: Keeping harmful substances out of drains reduces what water systems must handle.

questions.prompt: What is a key difference between wastewater and stormwater in many communities?
choices: Stormwater usually receives the same treatment as wastewater.
Wastewater is less likely to contain pollutants because it comes from buildings.
Stormwater mostly carries rainwater, so street pollutants are a minor concern.
Wastewater often goes to treatment, while stormwater may flow directly into local waterways.
questions.explanation: Storm drains can carry runoff directly to rivers or lakes without treatment.

questions.prompt: Why can even treated wastewater still affect ecosystems?
choices: Treatment adds chemicals mainly to make pollution easier to ignore.
Treatment can reduce pollutants but may leave some dissolved or suspended chemicals.
Treatment means the water becomes chemically pure.
Treated water is usually stored away from ecosystems permanently.
questions.explanation: Treatment systems vary and cannot always remove all chemicals.

questions.prompt: A person dumps oily liquid into a storm drain. What is the likely concern?
choices: It may enter the wastewater plant before reaching a nearby waterway.
It will dilute right away and become chemically pure.
It may be carried untreated into a local lake or river.
It will stay in the drain until maintenance workers remove it.
questions.explanation: Storm drains often connect directly to local waterways, making dumping especially harmful.

kp-178.title: KP4: Safe drinking water and community responsibility

questions.prompt: Why is safe drinking water a community responsibility?
choices: Water quality depends on infrastructure, pollution choices, monitoring, and public action.
Individual taste can help decide whether water needs testing.
Most communities can use the same plan if the water source is similar.
Water treatment systems work best when public action is kept separate.
questions.explanation: Safe water depends on systems and shared decisions, not only individual behaviour.

questions.prompt: Which action could support cleaner water systems?
choices: Test water mainly when contamination is visible.
Use storm drains for disposal if the material is diluted first.
Assume boiling water solves the problem before identifying the contaminant.
Raise awareness and reduce pollutants entering drains and waterways.
questions.explanation: Community action can reduce pollution and support better water quality.

questions.prompt: Why should water-quality plans consider different communities' needs?
choices: Most communities have similar water infrastructure and maintenance needs.
Some communities face greater barriers to safe drinking water and need appropriate support.
Water quality problems are mainly an issue for large cities.
Unsafe drinking water is mainly a short-term inconvenience if bottled water is available.
questions.explanation: The textbook notes that safe drinking water issues affect communities differently, including some First Nations communities.

questions.prompt: Why are there different classes of fire extinguishers?
choices: Fires can be grouped mainly by flame size once oxygen is present.
The letters mostly show how much extinguishing chemical is inside.
Different fuel sources require different chemicals or methods to extinguish safely.
Fire extinguishers are chosen mainly by how close they are to the fire.
questions.explanation: Extinguishers are matched to fuel sources such as ordinary combustibles, flammable liquids, electrical equipment, metals, or cooking oils.

questions.prompt: Which fire class involves live electrical equipment?
choices: Class C.
Class A.
Class D.
Class K.
questions.explanation: Class C fires involve energized electrical equipment.

questions.prompt: Which fire class is associated with cooking oils and fats?
choices: Class C.
Class D.
Class A, because cooking often uses paper packaging.
Class K.
questions.explanation: Class K fires involve cooking media such as vegetable or animal oils and fats.

questions.prompt: Why should water not be used on a live electrical fire?
choices: Water cools electrical equipment enough to remove the hazard.
Water can conduct electricity and create a life-threatening shock hazard.
Electrical fires should be treated like ordinary combustibles once the flame starts.
Water is the familiar choice, so it is usually the safest first option.
questions.explanation: Using water on live electrical equipment can cause electrocution.

questions.prompt: Why can water be dangerous on a cooking oil fire?
choices: It cools oil gradually without changing how the fire spreads.
It turns the oil fire into steam before the oil can splash.
It can spread burning oil and cause splattering.
It works well when the cooking oil is in a small container.
questions.explanation: Water can spread oil-based fires and increase burn risk.

questions.prompt: A small fire starts in a computer lab near energized equipment. What should guide extinguisher choice?
choices: The fire class and label showing safe use for electrical fires.
The heaviest extinguisher available.
A water extinguisher because water is familiar.
The extinguisher nearest the door regardless of label.
questions.explanation: Extinguisher labels identify which fire classes they can safely address.

questions.prompt: What do the letters on a fire extinguisher label tell you?
choices: The extinguisher's brand popularity.
The temperature range the extinguisher was tested at.
Which classes of fire the extinguisher can be used on safely.
The number of people allowed in the room.
questions.explanation: Fire extinguisher labels use letters and symbols to show the fire classes they can safely address.

questions.prompt: Why should you read the label before using a fire extinguisher?
choices: Using the wrong extinguisher can spread the fire or create a new hazard.
Most extinguishers work similarly if the fire is still small.
Labels mainly help with storage and inspection.
Reading labels matters most after the fire has been controlled.
questions.explanation: The label helps match the extinguisher to the fuel source and hazard.

questions.prompt: A label shows Class C. What fire type is it safe for, if the label instructions are followed?
choices: Cooking oil fires, because they often occur near appliances.
Combustible metal fires, because they need special dry powder.
Most fire types, as long as the fire is small.
Fires involving energized electrical equipment.
questions.explanation: Class C refers to fires involving live electrical equipment.

kp-182.title: KP4: Chemical trade-offs in fire safety

questions.prompt: Why can fire-suppression chemicals involve trade-offs?
choices: They are useful for some fire classes but may leave cleanup or disposal problems.
They may put out fires effectively but still have health, cleanup, or environmental risks.
Their effectiveness means health and environmental effects are usually minor.
Their chemistry matters most during use, while disposal can be considered later.
questions.explanation: A chemical can be useful for fire safety while also requiring careful handling or disposal.

questions.prompt: Why might a country phase out a fire-suppression chemical such as a halon?
choices: Its fire-fighting effect is useful, but labels make it harder to store.
It works for one fire class, so alternatives are not worth comparing.
Its environmental or health risks may outweigh its benefits when safer alternatives exist.
It makes water safer to use on electrical fires.
questions.explanation: Phasing out a chemical can happen when evidence shows unacceptable risk and alternatives are available.

questions.prompt: Which question best evaluates a fire-suppression chemical?
choices: Does it work for the fire class, and what risks does it create during use and disposal?
Is it newer than the extinguisher chemical it replaces?
Can it be used on many fire classes even if the label is unclear?
Does it simplify cleanup enough that disposal risks can be reviewed later?
questions.explanation: Good evaluation considers effectiveness, safety, environmental effects, and disposal.

questions.prompt: What is included in a product's life cycle?
choices: The product's use by consumers and the final disposal stage.
The product's price, popularity, and how often people replace it.
The manufacturing and disposal stages, but not distribution or use.
Raw material extraction, processing, manufacture, distribution, use, recycling, and disposal.
questions.explanation: A life cycle analysis follows a product from raw materials through use and end-of-life.

questions.prompt: Why is life cycle thinking useful for synthetic products?
choices: It shows whether a synthetic product should be rejected before comparing benefits.
It reveals impacts that may occur before use and after disposal, not just during use.
It focuses on the product's use because manufacturing and waste are separate issues.
It uses opinions about convenience before gathering environmental evidence.
questions.explanation: Environmental and social impacts can occur at many stages of a product's life.

questions.prompt: Which life cycle question fits a smartphone investigation?
choices: Which feature is most popular with the phone's current owner?
How many apps are installed compared with a previous phone?
What materials are extracted, how is it manufactured, and what happens when it is discarded?
What colour case reduces the chance that the phone will be replaced?
questions.explanation: Life cycle analysis asks about materials, manufacturing, use, recycling, and disposal.

kp-184.title: KP2: Positive and negative product impacts

questions.prompt: Which statement best describes many synthetic products?
choices: They can be useful and durable, but may also create waste, pollution, or toxicity concerns.
Their usefulness usually outweighs environmental questions without further evidence.
Their human-made design means safety concerns have already been solved.
Their environmental impact is mainly limited to the time when people use them.
questions.explanation: Synthetic products often involve both benefits and unintended consequences.

questions.prompt: A plastic bottle is lightweight and convenient but may persist as waste. What kind of analysis is this?
choices: Classifying the bottle's material without considering disposal.
Reading a recycling symbol without comparing benefits and waste.
Balancing the chemical equation for making the plastic.
Comparing positive and negative product impacts.
questions.explanation: The example weighs usefulness against disposal and pollution concerns.

questions.prompt: Which evidence would help decide whether a product is 'worth it'?
choices: A product slogan that describes the intended benefit.
Data about usefulness, lifetime, resource use, waste, toxicity, and alternatives.
One person's experience with the product's convenience.
The product's packaging style and whether it looks durable.
questions.explanation: A fair evaluation needs evidence about benefits, costs, and consequences.

questions.prompt: How can recycling reduce a product's negative impacts?
choices: It makes products easier to dispose of, even if collection systems are limited.
It reduces manufacturing impacts enough that raw materials matter less.
It can keep useful materials in use and reduce the need for new raw materials.
It works best when consumers separate materials, even without processing systems.
questions.explanation: Recycling can reduce some impacts, but it still requires systems and energy.

questions.prompt: What is a green alternative?
choices: A product or process designed to reduce environmental harm compared with another option.
A product with natural-looking packaging and a sustainability claim.
A product whose name suggests nature more clearly than its ingredients do.
A product that should be accepted before checking its full life cycle.
questions.explanation: Green alternatives aim to reduce harm, but they still need life cycle evaluation.

questions.prompt: Why should a 'green' claim still be checked?
choices: Green claims may focus on one benefit while leaving out other impacts.
Green claims are usually enough when the product replaces a less efficient option.
Life cycle evidence matters less for alternatives than for the original product.
The alternative may reduce one impact but create another impact elsewhere in its life cycle.
questions.explanation: Evidence helps determine whether an alternative truly reduces overall harm.

questions.prompt: Why is emergency preparedness important for chemical accidents?
choices: Accidents can be handled if roles are assigned as soon as the event starts.
Planning can reduce harm to people and the environment before an accident occurs.
Planning removes the need for cleanup if people evacuate quickly.
Preparedness matters most for large communities, not smaller workplaces.
questions.explanation: Preparedness helps people respond quickly and safely during emergencies.

questions.prompt: Which scenario belongs in a chemical emergency plan?
choices: Choosing a new meeting location for a regular club activity.
Graphing class trait variation for a genetics activity.
A transportation accident causing a hazardous chemical spill.
Naming a compound during a chemistry worksheet.
questions.explanation: Chemical spills, gas leaks, fires, and evacuation needs are relevant emergency scenarios.

questions.prompt: What is a common mistake in emergency planning?
choices: Assuming people will know what to do without practice or communication.
Identifying exits and meeting places.
Considering cleanup and disposal.
Assigning roles to responsible people.
questions.explanation: Plans must be communicated and practised so people can act under stress.

questions.prompt: Which item should be part of an emergency response plan?
choices: A list of materials involved without response roles or routes.
A list of hazards with cleanup decisions left until after evacuation.
The names of people on site without assigned responsibilities.
Evacuation routes, communication steps, cleanup methods, and disposal procedures.
questions.explanation: Effective plans include preparation, roles, evacuation, communication, cleanup, and disposal.

questions.prompt: Why should a plan state who is responsible for each action?
choices: Roles are useful mainly after first responders arrive.
Clear roles reduce confusion during an emergency.
People on site can choose roles during the event if the plan is clear.
First responders need details, while people on site mainly need the alarm.
questions.explanation: People need to know what actions to take and who coordinates them.

questions.prompt: A plan includes evacuation but says nothing about spilled chemical cleanup. What is missing?
choices: A communication method for telling people to leave.
A diagram showing the building's normal classroom layout.
A way to manage contamination and dispose of affected material safely.
A list of who will present the plan to the class.
questions.explanation: Chemical emergencies may require safe cleanup and disposal to protect people and the environment.

questions.prompt: Why should an emergency plan be tailored to its audience?
choices: Students, workers, families, and first responders may need different formats and details.
A single technical version is usually clearer for any audience.
Audience matters less than including a long technical explanation.
Plans should be written mainly for the people designing the response.
questions.explanation: Emergency information must be understandable and useful for the people who need to follow it.

questions.prompt: Which format could help communicate evacuation routes in a school?
choices: A paragraph describing exits without showing the building layout.
A list of room numbers without routes or meeting areas.
A poster focused on the chemical hazard but not evacuation.
A clear map showing exits, routes, and meeting areas.
questions.explanation: Maps and diagrams can show people where to go during an emergency.

questions.prompt: What should communication during an emergency be?
choices: Detailed enough that messages are sent after each person chooses a route.
Clear, accurate, and directed through known lines of communication.
Customized separately for each person, even if the shared procedure becomes unclear.
Delayed until the person in charge has gathered more background details.
questions.explanation: Clear communication helps people respond safely and consistently.

questions.prompt: What is a compound?
choices: A mixture of elements that are not chemically combined.
A substance made from one element written with a formula.
A substance made of atoms of different elements chemically combined in specific proportions.
A sample where different elements are close together but not bonded.
questions.explanation: Compounds contain different elements bonded in fixed ratios.

questions.prompt: Which substance is a compound?
choices: Water, H2O.
Oxygen gas, O2.
Copper metal, Cu.
Helium gas, He.
questions.explanation: Water contains hydrogen and oxygen chemically combined, so it is a compound.

questions.prompt: Why should you identify whether a substance is an element or compound before analyzing a reaction?
choices: It changes the mass of the atoms.
It lets you ignore chemical formulas.
It shows that formulas are less important than visible properties.
It helps track which atoms are rearranged into products.
questions.explanation: Chemical reactions rearrange atoms from elements and compounds into new substances.

questions.prompt: What particles make up ionic compounds?
choices: Neutral atoms arranged side by side without forming ions.
Positive cations and negative anions.
Separate molecules made by shared electron pairs.
Neutral atoms sharing electrons in small independent units.
questions.explanation: Ionic compounds consist of oppositely charged ions.

questions.prompt: Which example best represents an ionic compound?
choices: Oxygen gas, O2.
Hydrogen gas, H2.
Sodium chloride, made of Na+ and Cl- ions.
Carbon dioxide, because it contains two non-metals.
questions.explanation: Sodium chloride is composed of sodium cations and chloride anions in a crystal lattice.

questions.prompt: Why must the total positive and negative charges balance in an ionic compound?
choices: The compound as a whole is electrically neutral.
Ionic compounds need more positive ions than negative ions to hold together.
Anions become neutral atoms after the lattice forms.
Charge balance matters mainly for naming, not for the compound structure.
questions.explanation: Formula units show the smallest whole-number ratio of ions that gives overall neutrality.

questions.prompt: What is an ionic bond?
choices: A pair of atoms sharing electrons equally.
An attraction between neutral molecules after they share electrons.
A strong attraction between oppositely charged ions.
A bond between non-metals that is stronger when electrons are shared.
questions.explanation: Ionic bonds are electrostatic attractions between cations and anions.

questions.prompt: In a binary ionic compound, what usually happens between a metal atom and a non-metal atom?
choices: The metal loses electrons and the non-metal gains electrons.
Both atoms share electrons equally.
Both atoms become negative ions.
Both atoms stop having valence shells.
questions.explanation: Electron transfer forms a positive metal ion and a negative non-metal ion.

questions.prompt: Why does electron transfer often increase stability?
choices: The atoms remove their valence shells completely.
The ions become unrelated to charge.
The compound becomes a separate molecule.
The ions can achieve full valence shells.
questions.explanation: Full valence shells are associated with greater stability.

questions.prompt: How do covalent compounds form?
choices: Metal atoms transfer electrons to non-metals to make charged ions.
Non-metal atoms share valence electrons.
Ions arrange in a lattice after electrons are transferred.
Atoms form bonds without electrons.
questions.explanation: Covalent compounds form when non-metal atoms share electrons.

questions.prompt: Which substance is most likely covalent?
choices: Sodium chloride, NaCl.
Magnesium oxide, MgO.
Carbon dioxide, CO2.
Calcium fluoride, CaF2.
questions.explanation: Carbon and oxygen are non-metals, so carbon dioxide is covalent.

questions.prompt: What is the key difference between covalent and ionic bonding?
choices: Covalent bonding shares electrons; ionic bonding involves attraction between ions after electron transfer.
Covalent bonding uses metals when the compound has a familiar formula.
Ionic bonding forms separate molecules when the ion ratio is simple.
Covalent bonding has no valence electrons.
questions.explanation: The bonding models differ by electron sharing versus electron transfer and ion attraction.

questions.prompt: What is a covalent bond?
choices: An attraction between cations and anions after electron transfer.
A shared pair of electrons between two non-metal atoms.
A formula unit in a crystal lattice.
A strong attraction formed when atoms share valence electrons.
questions.explanation: Covalent bonds involve shared electron pairs attracted to atomic nuclei.

questions.prompt: What does a double covalent bond represent?
choices: Two formula units in an ionic lattice.
Two shared pairs of electrons between atoms.
Two ions attracted in a crystal lattice.
Two atoms sharing one pair of electrons twice as strongly.
questions.explanation: A double bond means the atoms share two pairs of electrons.

questions.prompt: In a Lewis diagram, what does a single line between two atoms usually represent?
choices: One transferred electron from a metal atom.
One formula unit in an ionic lattice.
One shared pair of electrons.
One atom that is no longer counted in the formula.
questions.explanation: A line in a Lewis diagram represents a bonding pair of electrons.

questions.prompt: What is a molecule?
choices: A particle made of two or more atoms bonded by covalent bonds.
A crystal lattice of positive and negative ions.
A charged ion that represents the smallest part of an ionic compound.
A formula unit that repeats through an ionic crystal.
questions.explanation: Molecules are covalently bonded groups of atoms.

questions.prompt: Which is a molecule but not a compound?
choices: Water, H2O.
Sodium chloride, NaCl.
Magnesium oxide, MgO.
Oxygen gas, O2.
questions.explanation: O2 is a molecule because oxygen atoms are covalently bonded, but it is not a compound because it has one element.

questions.prompt: Why is water described as molecular?
choices: It is a continuous ionic lattice.
It exists as independent H2O molecules held together by covalent bonds within each molecule.
It contains a repeated ion ratio like sodium chloride.
It is described by a formula unit rather than independent particles.
questions.explanation: Water is a covalent compound whose smallest independent units are molecules.

questions.prompt: Why is sodium chloride usually described by formula units rather than molecules?
choices: It has no ions.
It is made of shared electron pairs between sodium and chlorine.
It forms a continuous ionic lattice instead of separate NaCl molecules.
It forms small NaCl molecules that pack tightly into a crystal.
questions.explanation: Ionic compounds are represented by the smallest ratio of ions, called a formula unit.

questions.prompt: What does the formula unit NaCl show?
choices: A 1:1 ratio of sodium ions to chloride ions.
One separate covalent molecule with shared electrons.
A 2:1 ratio because sodium has the lower atomic mass.
A compound with no charge balance.
questions.explanation: NaCl shows the simplest whole-number ratio needed for a neutral ionic compound.

questions.prompt: Which statement avoids a common mistake?
choices: A simple chemical formula usually means a substance is a separate molecule.
Ionic compounds are best pictured as neutral atom pairs.
Covalent compounds use formula units when they have more than two atoms.
Ionic compounds have formula units; many covalent substances have molecules.
questions.explanation: Formula units and molecules describe different kinds of structures.

questions.prompt: What happens to atoms during a chemical reaction?
choices: They keep the same bonding arrangements but are renamed as products.
They are rearranged into new substances.
They change into different elements when products form.
They are replaced by product atoms after bonds break.
questions.explanation: Chemical reactions rearrange existing atoms into products.

questions.prompt: Methane reacts with oxygen to form carbon dioxide and water. What should be tracked to understand the reaction?
choices: The colour and temperature of the flame before considering formulas.
The container shape, because it controls which atoms are produced.
Where each carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen atom goes in the products.
The names of the reactants, because formulas give less useful atom information.
questions.explanation: Tracking atoms shows how reactants are rearranged into products.

questions.prompt: Which statement is correct for a chemical reaction?
choices: Reactant atoms are conserved but bonded in new ways.
Product atoms are added as reactant bonds release energy.
Reactant atoms become energy when old bonds break.
Atoms matter less than colour change when identifying products.
questions.explanation: Atoms are rearranged; later this connects to conservation of mass.

questions.prompt: Why must some chemical bonds be broken during a reaction?
choices: Bonds must be broken so atoms can change into different elements.
New products form when old bonds stretch but stay connected.
Bonds matter mainly for naming, not for forming products.
Atoms must separate from old arrangements before new arrangements can form.
questions.explanation: Reactant bonds are broken so atoms can rearrange.

questions.prompt: What is required to break a chemical bond?
choices: Energy is released as soon as old bonds start to separate.
Energy must be added.
Energy is not needed if the reaction later releases heat.
A catalyst label can replace the energy needed to separate bonded atoms.
questions.explanation: Breaking the electrostatic attraction in a bond requires energy input.

questions.prompt: What does the amount of energy needed to break a bond depend on?
choices: Whether the bond is shown in a large or small diagram.
The amount of product that will be collected later.
The atoms or ions involved in the bond.
Whether the compound name is short or familiar.
questions.explanation: Different bonds have different strengths depending on the particles involved.

questions.prompt: What happens after atoms are rearranged in a chemical reaction?
choices: New bonds form to create product substances.
Atoms remain separated until another reactant is added later.
Energy is no longer involved once the old bonds have broken.
Reactant bonds reform unless the products are gases.
questions.explanation: Products form when rearranged atoms bond in new combinations.

questions.prompt: What happens to energy when a chemical bond forms?
choices: Energy must be absorbed because atoms are moving closer together.
No energy change occurs.
Energy is stored by removing mass from the atoms.
Energy is released.
questions.explanation: Bond formation releases energy as atoms move to a more stable arrangement.

questions.prompt: Which observation could suggest that new products formed?
choices: The substances are simply moved to a new beaker.
A new gas, solid, colour, or other property appears after reactants interact.
A solid is crushed so it has a different shape.
The same mixture is stirred until it looks more uniform.
questions.explanation: New properties can indicate that new substances and bonds have formed.

questions.prompt: Which statement about bond energy is correct?
choices: Breaking bonds releases energy because atoms move apart.
Both breaking and forming bonds require energy input.
Breaking bonds requires energy, while forming bonds releases energy.
Energy changes happen after products form, not during bonding.
questions.explanation: This is the key energy pattern for chemical bonds.

questions.prompt: A reaction releases more energy when new bonds form than it absorbs breaking old bonds. What is the overall result?
choices: Energy is released overall.
Energy is absorbed overall.
No products can form.
The products must have the same bonds as the reactants.
questions.explanation: The net energy change depends on the balance between energy absorbed and released.

questions.prompt: Why do fireworks involve energy changes?
choices: Atoms in fireworks are not rearranged.
Fireworks mainly show heated materials glowing without new substances.
Chemical bonds do not store or release energy.
Bonds in reactants break and new bonds form in products, producing heat, gas, and light.
questions.explanation: Fireworks are dramatic examples of chemical reactions with energy changes.

questions.prompt: Why do atoms form ionic or covalent bonds?
choices: Atoms bond to increase instability on purpose.
Bonding can help atoms achieve more stable valence electron arrangements.
Atoms bond mainly to change the number of protons in their nuclei.
Bonding is related to atom size but not to valence electrons.
questions.explanation: Atoms often become more stable when valence shells are filled through transfer or sharing of electrons.

questions.prompt: How can a non-metal atom achieve a full valence shell in a covalent compound?
choices: By losing protons until it has a full outer shell.
By transferring its valence electrons to a metal atom.
By sharing valence electrons with another non-metal atom.
By keeping its valence electrons separate from nearby atoms.
questions.explanation: Covalent bonding fills valence shells through shared electron pairs.

questions.prompt: How can a metal atom become more stable in a binary ionic compound?
choices: By losing valence electrons to form a cation with a stable electron arrangement.
By sharing electrons equally with another metal atom.
By gaining enough electrons to become a large anion.
By keeping its valence electrons while the non-metal changes charge.
questions.explanation: Metals often lose valence electrons, while non-metals gain them, forming stable ions.

kp-201.title: KP1: Law of conservation of mass

questions.prompt: A sealed flask contains 42.0 g of reactants before a reaction. After the reaction is complete, what total mass should be in the flask?
choices: Less than 42.0 g if a gas forms inside the sealed flask
More than 42.0 g if the products are denser than the reactants
A different mass if the reaction gives off heat
questions.explanation: In a chemical reaction, mass is conserved when matter cannot leave or enter the system.

questions.prompt: Which statement best describes the law of conservation of mass?
choices: The total mass of substances used equals the total mass of substances produced.
Reactants and products have equal mass when gases are not produced.
Products can have more measured mass if heat is released in a closed system.
Mass is conserved when the reaction is balanced by changing formulas.
questions.explanation: Chemical reactions rearrange atoms; they do not create or destroy matter.

questions.prompt: Why is atom rearrangement important for explaining conservation of mass?
choices: Atoms leave the equation when reactant bonds break.
Energy conservation explains mass without tracking atoms.
Product atoms are supplied by heat released during the reaction.
The same atoms are present before and after the reaction, just connected differently.
questions.explanation: Conservation of mass follows from conserving the number and type of atoms.

questions.prompt: Which setup is best for testing whether mass is conserved during a reaction that produces a gas?
choices: An open beaker so gas can leave without changing pressure.
A sealed container on a balance before and after mixing the reactants.
A paper cup weighed after bubbling stops because the reaction is complete.
A container opened briefly after the reaction so the final products can settle.
questions.explanation: A closed system prevents matter, including gases, from leaving or entering.

questions.prompt: In a closed system, what can enter or leave while matter cannot?
choices: Atoms
Gas products
Energy
Solid precipitates
questions.explanation: Closed systems can exchange energy with the surroundings, but not matter.

questions.prompt: A student seals a flask, mixes two solutions inside it, and finds the mass is unchanged. What does this result support?
choices: The reaction conserved mass because no matter left the system.
No gas could have formed during the reaction.
The products must have the same properties as the reactants.
The balance measured energy instead of matter.
questions.explanation: Mass can remain constant even when new substances form, if the system is closed.

questions.prompt: An effervescent tablet reacts in an open beaker of water and the measured mass decreases. What is the best explanation?
choices: Gas formed but stayed dissolved, so the system lost mass by becoming less dense.
The water absorbed the missing mass as heat.
The balance reads lower because the product is less dense.
Gas particles escaped into the air, so the beaker system lost matter.
questions.explanation: In an open system, matter such as gas can leave, changing the measured mass of the container.

questions.prompt: Which description matches an open system?
choices: Matter stays inside while energy exchange is the main change.
Matter and energy can both enter or leave.
Energy can enter, but gas products are expected to stay inside.
Matter can leave, but energy is trapped inside.
questions.explanation: Open systems allow exchange of both matter and energy with surroundings.

questions.prompt: Why can an open-system reaction seem to violate conservation of mass?
choices: Chemical reactions in open containers seem conserved when no bubbles are visible.
Open containers prevent accurate product formation when gases are involved.
Matter may move out of the measured system even though total matter is still conserved.
Energy leaving the system is usually measured as missing product mass.
questions.explanation: The apparent change comes from the boundary of what is measured, not from matter being destroyed.

questions.prompt: Why were careful mass measurements important in Lavoisier-style experiments?
choices: They showed that the mass of reactants matched the mass of products in closed systems.
They showed that gases could be ignored if the solid was weighed carefully.
They showed that heating a solid usually increases its mass by adding energy.
They replaced the need to track whether matter entered or left the system.
questions.explanation: Repeated measurements helped establish the law of conservation of mass.

questions.prompt: A scientist heats a solid in sealed glassware and measures all contents before and after. What makes this evidence strong?
choices: Heating adds mass to the chemicals being tested.
Glassware makes the colour change easier to compare with mass change.
The colour of a product can stand in for measuring the substances.
The system prevents products such as gases from escaping during measurement.
questions.explanation: Keeping matter inside the apparatus lets the measured mass represent all reactants and products.

questions.prompt: Which conclusion best connects experimental evidence to a scientific law?
choices: One dramatic reaction is enough if the mass change is easy to see.
Repeated closed-system reactions with equal before-and-after mass support a general conservation rule.
If a reaction gives off light, the energy evidence is more useful than mass evidence.
A law is based on a useful explanation before repeated measurement is needed.
questions.explanation: Scientific laws are supported by repeated patterns in evidence.

questions.prompt: Which atom count shows that \(2CO + O_2 -> 2CO_2\) conserves oxygen atoms?
choices: Left: 2 O atoms; right: 4 O atoms
Left: 4 O atoms; right: 4 O atoms
Left: 3 O atoms; right: 2 O atoms
Left: 1 O atom; right: 2 O atoms
questions.explanation: The left side has 2 O atoms in 2CO plus 2 O atoms in O2, for 4 total; the right side has 4 O atoms in 2CO2.

questions.prompt: Which equation is not balanced because oxygen atoms are not conserved?
questions.explanation: Hydrogen is conserved in this equation, but oxygen is not; it is still the unbalanced choice among the options.

questions.prompt: What should you count to check whether an equation obeys conservation of mass?
choices: The number of plus signs on each side.
The number of reactant formulas compared with product formulas.
The number of compound names on each side.
Atoms of each element on both sides of the arrow.
questions.explanation: A balanced equation has the same number of each kind of atom on both sides.

questions.prompt: What does a chemical equation represent?
choices: The visible signs of a reaction without showing reactants.
A chemical reaction using words, formulas, or both.
A list of substances before atom counts are considered.
A measurement table for temperature during an experiment.
questions.explanation: Chemical equations summarize reactants changing into products.

questions.prompt: In a chemical equation, what does the arrow usually mean?
choices: Has more mass than
Must be balanced before
Produces or yields
Has the same formula as
questions.explanation: The arrow points from reactants toward the products formed.

questions.prompt: In \(zinc + hydrochloric\ acid -> zinc\ chloride + hydrogen\), what does the plus sign on the left mean?
choices: Zinc reacts with hydrochloric acid.
Zinc is added after hydrochloric acid forms.
Hydrochloric acid is listed as a product.
Zinc and hydrochloric acid have already become one substance.
questions.explanation: On the reactant side, the plus sign means the substances react with each other.

questions.prompt: In \(2Mg + O_2 -> 2MgO\), which substance is a product?
choices: \(MgO\)
questions.explanation: Products are written on the right side of the arrow.

questions.prompt: What are reactants?
choices: Substances shown after the arrow in the equation.
Substances that undergo chemical change during a reaction.
Substances formed when the arrow points to the right.
Numbers placed before formulas to show reaction ratios.
questions.explanation: Reactants start on the left side and are changed into products.

questions.prompt: A student writes calcium carbonate -> calcium oxide + carbon dioxide. Which statement is correct?
choices: The substances after the arrow are reactants in the second step.
Carbon dioxide is the reactant because gases are usually written last.
Calcium carbonate is the reactant; calcium oxide and carbon dioxide are products.
The arrow points from products to reactants.
questions.explanation: The substance before the arrow reacts, and the substances after the arrow form.

questions.prompt: Which is a word equation for iron reacting with sulfur to form iron sulfide?
choices: iron + sulfur -> iron sulfide
\(Fe + S -> FeS\)
iron sulfide + energy -> iron + sulfur
questions.explanation: A word equation uses substance names instead of chemical formulas.

questions.prompt: Why can a word equation be useful before writing formulas?
choices: It shows the reaction is balanced once the substance names are in the right order.
It gives atom counts before chemical formulas are written.
It replaces the need to write formulas when coefficients are required.
It identifies the reactants and products in words first.
questions.explanation: Word equations help organize the reaction before translating names into formulas.

questions.prompt: Which information is usually missing from a simple word equation?
choices: Names of reactants and products
Exact chemical formulas and coefficients
The direction from reactants to products
Whether a reaction is being described
questions.explanation: Word equations name substances but usually do not show formulas or balanced ratios.

questions.prompt: What makes \(Mg + O_2 -> MgO\) a skeleton equation rather than a balanced equation?
choices: It uses substance names instead of formulas.
It has formulas and products, but not the correct atom ratios.
It uses formulas but does not show equal oxygen atom counts on both sides.
It contains state symbols.
questions.explanation: A skeleton equation gives formulas but may not yet conserve atoms.

questions.prompt: Which statement about skeleton equations is correct?
choices: They may need coefficients added to obey conservation of mass.
They are balanced once the formulas are correct.
They change formulas by altering subscripts.
They use formulas and state symbols but not reaction arrows.
questions.explanation: Skeleton equations are starting points for balancing.

questions.prompt: A skeleton equation has correct formulas but unequal atom counts. What should be changed?
choices: Subscripts inside formulas
Element symbols so atom counts match faster
Reactants into substances with simpler formulas
Coefficients in front of formulas
questions.explanation: Balancing changes coefficients, not the identities of substances.

questions.prompt: Which equation is balanced?
choices: \(Na + Cl_2 -> NaCl\)
\(2Na + Cl_2 -> 2NaCl\)
\(Na + Cl -> NaCl_2\)
\(2Na + Cl_2 -> Na_2Cl\)
questions.explanation: The balanced equation has 2 Na atoms and 2 Cl atoms on both sides.

questions.prompt: What must be true in a balanced chemical equation?
choices: The products must have fewer atoms than the reactants.
The coefficients must be the same as the subscripts.
Each element has the same number of atoms on both sides.
The reactants and products must have the same physical state.
questions.explanation: Balanced equations show conservation of atoms and mass.

questions.prompt: Which final check best confirms that \(N_2 + 3H_2 -> 2NH_3\) is balanced?
choices: Count 2 N atoms and 6 H atoms on both sides.
Count two formulas on the left and one formula on the right.
Check that nitrogen is written before hydrogen on both sides.
Make sure state symbols do not affect the coefficients.
questions.explanation: The atom counts for every element must match.

questions.prompt: In \(2Ca_3(PO_4)_2\), which atom count correctly uses the coefficient?
choices: 3 calcium atoms, 2 phosphorus atoms, and 8 oxygen atoms.
2 calcium atoms, 3 phosphorus atoms, and 4 oxygen atoms.
6 calcium atoms, 4 phosphorus atoms, and 16 oxygen atoms.
6 calcium atoms, 2 phosphorus atoms, and 8 oxygen atoms.
questions.explanation: The coefficient multiplies the whole formula: 2 x Ca3 gives 6 Ca, 2 x P2 gives 4 P, and 2 x O8 gives 16 O.

questions.prompt: A student places a 2 in front of \(MgO\) in \(Mg + O_2 -> MgO\). What does that coefficient mean?
choices: There are two formula units of magnesium oxide, so both Mg and O are doubled.
The coefficient changes the oxygen count but leaves magnesium unchanged.
The formula becomes \(Mg_2O\).
The coefficient changes the subscript on oxygen when atoms are counted.
questions.explanation: A coefficient applies to the entire formula after it; it changes the amount, not the formula itself.

questions.prompt: Why are coefficients used instead of changing formulas when balancing a chemical equation?
choices: They let atom counts be adjusted without changing the chemical formula.
They show which reactant has the greatest number of atoms in one formula unit.
They turn an unbalanced formula equation back into a word equation.
They adjust the number of particles while keeping each substance's identity the same.
questions.explanation: Balancing changes the amounts of substances. Changing a formula would describe a different substance.

questions.prompt: In \(Al_2(SO_4)_3\), what does the subscript 3 outside the brackets mean?
choices: The sulfur atoms are tripled, while oxygen is counted from the original group.
The whole sulfate group is counted three times in one formula unit.
There are three formula units of aluminum sulfate.
The subscript changes the coefficient needed to balance the equation.
questions.explanation: A subscript outside brackets multiplies every atom inside the bracketed group.

questions.prompt: Which change would alter the identity of a substance rather than just the amount present?
choices: Changing \(CO_2\) to \(2CO_2\).
Changing \(Cl_2\) to \(3Cl_2\).
Changing \(H_2O\) to \(H_2O_2\).
Changing \(NaCl\) to \(4NaCl\).
questions.explanation: Changing a subscript changes the atom ratio inside one formula unit, so it describes a different substance.

questions.prompt: How many oxygen atoms are represented by \(3Ca(NO_3)_2\)?
questions.explanation: One formula unit has 2 nitrate groups, each with 3 oxygen atoms. The coefficient 3 gives 3 x 2 x 3 = 18 oxygen atoms.

questions.prompt: Which statement correctly distinguishes \((aq)\) from \((l)\) in a chemical equation?
choices: \((aq)\) means the substance is a liquid element, while \((l)\) means it is dissolved.
Both symbols mean liquid water is being produced.
\((aq)\) means the substance is mixed with water, while \((l)\) means it is any wet substance.
\((aq)\) means dissolved in water, while \((l)\) means the pure substance is liquid.
questions.explanation: Aqueous describes a substance dissolved in water; liquid describes the physical state of the pure substance.

questions.prompt: Which equation fragment correctly shows solid zinc reacting with hydrochloric acid solution?
choices: \(Zn(aq) + HCl(s)\)
\(Zn(s) + HCl(aq)\)
\(Zn(l) + HCl(aq)\)
\(Zn(s) + HCl(l)\)
questions.explanation: A metal sample is shown as solid, and an acid solution is aqueous.

questions.prompt: A reaction between two clear solutions forms a cloudy solid. Why would state symbols matter in the equation?
choices: They prove that the equation is balanced without checking atoms.
They replace coefficients when a solid forms.
They show that dissolved reactants can form an insoluble solid product.
They show which side has more atoms after the solid appears.
questions.explanation: State symbols can show aqueous reactants and a solid precipitate, adding useful evidence about what changed.

questions.prompt: Which formula correctly represents elemental chlorine in a chemical equation?
questions.explanation: Chlorine is one of the elements that naturally occurs as a diatomic molecule.

questions.prompt: Which pair correctly includes one diatomic elemental form and one polyatomic elemental form from this topic?
questions.explanation: Bromine is diatomic as \(Br_2\), while sulfur is commonly represented as the polyatomic element \(S_8\).

questions.prompt: In \(N_2 + 3H_2 -> 2NH_3\), why is nitrogen written as \(N_2\) instead of \(N\)?
choices: The coefficient 3 on hydrogen forces nitrogen to be written with a subscript.
Elemental nitrogen naturally exists as two-atom molecules.
A subscript is added because nitrogen is written before hydrogen.
Nitrogen becomes a compound when it reacts with hydrogen.
questions.explanation: Some elements, including nitrogen and hydrogen, must be written as diatomic molecules when they appear as elements.

questions.prompt: When beginning to balance a chemical equation, which move best matches the textbook strategy?
choices: Change the formula of the compound with the most atoms first.
Start with hydrogen and oxygen first because they are common in equations.
Check that formulas are correct, then balance compounds and leave elements such as H and O until later when helpful.
Use larger coefficients at the start and reduce them if the equation looks complicated.
questions.explanation: The textbook strategy emphasizes coefficients, compounds first, and a final atom check.

questions.prompt: Which balanced equation uses the smallest whole-number coefficients for iron reacting with chlorine to form iron(III) chloride?
choices: \(Fe + Cl_2 -> FeCl_3\)
questions.explanation: The coefficients 2, 3, and 2 give 2 Fe atoms and 6 Cl atoms on each side, and the ratio cannot be reduced.

questions.prompt: In \(Pb(NO_3)_2 + KI -> PbI_2 + KNO_3\), why can nitrate be counted as a unit while balancing?
choices: Nitrate has no atoms that affect conservation of mass.
Any aqueous ion can be ignored when balancing a reaction.
The subscript on nitrate must be changed before coefficients are placed.
The \(NO_3\) group appears unchanged on both sides, so tracking it as a group can simplify the count.
questions.explanation: Unchanged polyatomic ions can be counted as units to simplify balancing.

questions.prompt: An incinerator turns a bag of waste into ash and gases. What does conservation of mass suggest?
choices: The ash is the main matter left because gases spread out and are harder to collect.
The original atoms are now distributed among ash and gases rather than disappearing.
The mass law stops applying when a reaction happens outside a lab.
The missing solid mass has mostly become heat rather than matter.
questions.explanation: Waste can be transformed into other substances or spread into the surroundings, but its atoms are still conserved.

questions.prompt: In an open beaker, a fizzy tablet reaction seems to lose mass over time. What is the best interpretation?
choices: Some atoms left the measured system as gas instead of disappearing.
The balance reading changes because bubbles push upward on the beaker.
Gas likely escaped into the surroundings, so the measured system lost matter.
The tablet stopped being matter after dissolving.
questions.explanation: In an open system, gas products can leave the measured system even though total matter is conserved overall.

questions.prompt: A company says a pollutant is no longer a concern because it was diluted in a large volume of water. What is the key science concern?
choices: Dilution lowers concentration, but the pollutant matter still exists and may move through the environment.
Dilution increases the total amount of pollutant atoms by spreading them out.
Water lowers concentration enough that the pollutant atoms can be ignored.
Dissolved substances stop obeying conservation of mass.
questions.explanation: Conservation of mass helps explain why spreading material out is not the same as eliminating it.

questions.prompt: Which choice is the clearest example of reducing waste before it is created?
choices: Sorting plastic containers after a picnic.
Melting used aluminum cans into new sheet metal.
Composting fruit peels after lunch.
Buying a durable lunch container instead of daily disposable packaging.
questions.explanation: Reducing prevents some material from entering the waste stream in the first place.

questions.prompt: Which statement best connects reducing waste to conservation of mass?
choices: Reducing matters after a product has already entered the waste stream.
Reducing lowers how much material must later be collected, processed, or released.
Reducing matters mainly for waste that is already classified as hazardous.
Reducing is useful because recycled matter eventually disappears.
questions.explanation: Because matter persists, preventing some material use can reduce the later handling problem.

questions.prompt: A school is comparing a bottle-refill program with a larger recycling bin. Which evidence best evaluates the reducing option?
choices: How many students notice the refill station sign.
Whether students can name the plastic recycling code on bottles.
How many single-use bottles are avoided before they become waste.
Whether bottles in the recycling bin are sorted neatly.
questions.explanation: Reduction is measured by material use prevented, not only by how existing waste is processed.

questions.prompt: Why is waste classification useful when making disposal decisions?
choices: It helps match handling and storage decisions to the waste's source, hazards, and possible movement.
It shows that residential waste is usually separate from hazardous-material decisions.
It removes the need to measure the amount of waste.
It changes chemical waste into ordinary garbage.
questions.explanation: Classifying waste helps guide decisions, but it does not remove the need to consider evidence and risk.

questions.prompt: What is a possible limitation of simply labelling waste by source, such as residential or industrial?
choices: The label gives a broad category but may not identify each chemical present.
The label usually gives enough detail to choose disposal without further evidence.
The label can make different items from one source seem more similar than they are.
The label may hide important differences in toxicity, concentration, or how the waste can spread.
questions.explanation: Classification helps, but decisions still require evidence about danger, amount, and movement.

questions.prompt: After a container is labelled hazardous waste, what question is still scientifically important?
choices: Whether the label category was assigned before or after storage.
What concentration, amount, reactivity, and exposure pathway are involved.
Whether the container came from a home or a school.
Whether the waste can be renamed as industrial waste instead.
questions.explanation: A hazard label is a starting point; risk decisions also depend on amount, concentration, and how people or ecosystems could be exposed.

questions.prompt: Why is tanning better described as chemical processing than just shaping a hide?
choices: The hide becomes useful mainly after it has been cut to the right shape.
Cutting the hide into a pattern is the main chemical change.
Materials added during tanning change properties such as decay resistance, flexibility, and water response.
The process works mainly because water is physically squeezed out of the hide.
questions.explanation: Tanning changes the material's properties, so it involves more than a physical change in shape.

questions.prompt: Which property would be most useful evidence that raw hide has been successfully treated?
choices: It remains flexible and resists decay better than untreated hide.
It absorbs water quickly and returns to raw hide when soaked.
It becomes stiff because the fibres are no longer affected by oils.
It keeps the same properties as raw hide but has a different colour.
questions.explanation: Tanning helps preserve hide and make it useful as leather.

questions.prompt: What is the strongest chemistry connection in traditional tanning knowledge?
choices: It depends mainly on the hide colour before treatment begins.
It is less chemical because the treatment materials are natural.
It uses smoke mainly to make the final product darker.
It applies substances and process conditions that change how hide fibres behave.
questions.explanation: Traditional methods can involve careful knowledge of material interactions, preservation, and fibre properties.

questions.prompt: What role do fats and oils from the brain emulsion play in brain tanning?
choices: They coat the hide but do not change how the fibres behave.
They help lubricate and alter hide fibres so the finished leather stays flexible.
They remove the need for any scraping, stretching, or smoking.
They replace water in the process but do not affect flexibility.
questions.explanation: Brain tissue can provide fats and oils that affect the hide's fibres.

questions.prompt: Which sequence best shows why brain tanning is a process rather than a single instant reaction?
choices: Scraping the hide, then assuming preservation follows from soaking alone.
Adding water and skipping stretching because oils do the chemical work.
Scraping and washing the hide, working in the emulsion, stretching it, and smoking it.
Smoking the hide first, then deciding whether chemical treatment is needed.
questions.explanation: The method includes several physical preparation steps and chemical treatment steps.

questions.prompt: Which comparison is most reasonable when evaluating brain tanning and industrial tanning?
choices: Brain tanning can use natural materials, while some industrial methods use chemicals that require careful waste and safety management.
Natural materials usually make scale and labour less important to sustainability.
Industrial methods are less chemical if machines do most of the work.
Both methods make waste matter disappear once the leather is finished.
questions.explanation: A fair comparison considers materials, toxicity, labour, scale, waste, and the fact that matter does not disappear.

questions.prompt: Which comparison best evaluates tanning methods using both sustainability and safety?
choices: Industrial tanning should be preferred if it produces leather quickly, even when waste handling is not described.
Traditional tanning should be preferred if ingredients are natural, even when labour and material supply are not considered.
Traditional methods may use more of the animal and natural materials, while some industrial methods may need controls for toxic chemicals and waste.
The safest comparison is to count how many chemical names appear in each procedure.
questions.explanation: A strong evaluation considers materials used, waste, safety, scale, and environmental impact.

questions.prompt: Why might a traditional method be difficult to use on a large commercial scale even if it is more natural?
choices: It may require more labour, time, specialized knowledge, and limited biological materials.
Scaling mainly requires larger containers; labour and material supply do not affect the evaluation.
Natural materials remove the need to measure waste, exposure, or consistency.
Large-scale production is sustainable if each hide has the same final texture.
questions.explanation: Sustainability decisions must include practicality as well as chemical and environmental risks.

questions.prompt: A company claims its tanning method is sustainable because it uses fewer synthetic chemicals. What question should still be asked?
choices: How much waste, energy use, worker exposure, and usable product does the whole process involve?
Whether the method avoids synthetic chemicals in the main recipe.
Whether the finished leather has a consistent colour and texture.
Whether the process can be advertised as traditional or natural.
questions.explanation: A sustainability claim should be checked across the whole process, not only one material choice.

questions.prompt: Why should a conservation-of-mass investigation measure the system both before and after a reaction?
choices: The first measurement identifies products before the reaction is observed.
The two measurements can be compared to test whether the defined system's mass changed.
