The second measurement replaces the need for a balanced equation.
Mass should be measured after gas escape so the final reading is stable.
questions.explanation: Before-and-after measurements let you compare the total mass of the reaction system.

questions.prompt: A sealed flask has a mass of 184.37 g before two solutions are mixed. After the reaction, it measures 184.36 g. What is the best interpretation?
choices: Some product may have stuck to the glass, so it no longer counts in the system mass.
The small decrease shows that the precipitate is lighter than the dissolved reactants.
The 0.01 g difference is likely measurement uncertainty, so mass is essentially conserved.
The reaction likely released enough energy to slightly lower the measured mass.
questions.explanation: Real measurements can vary slightly, so evidence should be interpreted with uncertainty in mind.

questions.prompt: Why compare a closed-system trial with an open-system trial?
choices: It helps show how escaping matter can affect measured mass even when total matter is conserved.
It separates evidence for a reaction from evidence about where matter can move.
It lets the same reaction be judged by observations as well as mass measurements.
It makes the open-system data easier to interpret when gas leaves the beaker.
questions.explanation: Open and closed systems help distinguish actual conservation from what the balance measures.

questions.prompt: In a closed-system reaction, why is a stopper or sealed lid important?
choices: It keeps matter such as gases, droplets, and solutions inside the measured system.
It keeps the reactants separated until the balance has finished measuring them.
It lets the observer see a precipitate without counting the container's mass.
It reduces evaporation, so the chemical reaction releases less energy.
questions.explanation: A sealed container keeps all matter inside the system being weighed.

questions.prompt: Two solutions react inside a sealed flask and form a cloudy solid. What should happen to the total mass of the sealed flask and contents?
choices: It should increase slightly because dissolved particles become visible.
It should remain the same within measurement uncertainty.
It should decrease if the cloudy solid settles to the bottom.
It should depend mainly on whether the solid is white or coloured.
questions.explanation: New substances can form while the total matter in the closed system remains constant.

questions.prompt: Which observation would be evidence of a reaction in a closed system without contradicting mass conservation?
choices: A precipitate forms while the sealed container's total mass stays nearly constant.
The mixture changes colour and the balance reading drops because energy was released.
Gas bubbles appear and are counted as evidence that mass has left the flask.
A new solid forms, so the products are weighed separately from the container.
questions.explanation: Chemical evidence and mass conservation can occur at the same time.

questions.prompt: A tablet bubbles in an open beaker of water and the balance reading decreases over several minutes. What most likely left the measured system?
choices: Some dissolved solid that settled below the water
Gas produced by the reaction
Some water molecules that became part of the tablet
Heat energy that made the solution feel cooler
questions.explanation: Bubbling can indicate gas production, and gas can escape from an open beaker.

questions.prompt: If the same bubbling reaction were done in a sealed container, what result would better show conservation of mass?
choices: The total mass would decrease more slowly because gas pressure builds up.
The tablet would dissolve but the gas would stop counting as matter.
The reaction would make gas, but the balance would measure less mass because bubbles rise.
The total mass of the sealed container and contents would stay nearly constant.
questions.explanation: Sealing the system keeps gas inside, so it remains part of the measured mass.

questions.prompt: Why is it misleading to say that mass was destroyed in an open beaker reaction?
choices: The gas bubbles make the solution less dense, so the balance reads a lower mass.
The missing measured mass may have moved into the surrounding air.
The balance reading decreases because the reaction releases energy as bubbles form.
The final beaker mass is lower because bubbles are lighter than liquid reactants.
questions.explanation: Open-system measurements can lose track of matter that leaves the container.

questions.prompt: Which observation would be good evidence that a chemical reaction occurred when two clear solutions were mixed?
choices: The mixture stayed clear but the total volume was recorded carefully.
The liquids were poured using the same graduated cylinder.
A new cloudy solid formed in the mixture.
The flask was stoppered before the two solutions were allowed to mix.
questions.explanation: Formation of a precipitate can indicate that new substances formed.

questions.prompt: Which set of observations should be recorded during a reaction investigation?
choices: Temperature changes, gas production, colour changes, precipitates, and mass readings
Initial and final masses, but not changes in temperature or appearance.
Visible changes and timing, but not mass readings from the balance.
Equipment used and final appearance, but not observations during the reaction.
questions.explanation: Multiple observations help connect evidence to whether a chemical reaction occurred.

questions.prompt: A reaction mixture becomes colder and produces bubbles. What should a careful student conclude?
choices: The gas production explains the temperature change, so mass data are not needed.
Bubbles prove the system was closed.
The temperature change matters, but bubbles are the stronger evidence by themselves.
These observations are evidence of a reaction and energy transfer, but the student should still record data carefully.
questions.explanation: Observations support a reaction claim, but good science still depends on careful data.

questions.prompt: Which everyday example shows matter and energy interacting during a physical change?
choices: A metal spoon is listed in an equipment table before the investigation begins.
Ice absorbs energy from warmer surroundings and melts.
Toast browns as new substances form on the surface of bread.
Propane reacts with oxygen after a spark starts the flame.
questions.explanation: Melting is a physical change that involves energy transfer.

questions.prompt: Which example shows matter and energy interacting during a chemical change?
choices: Ice melts in a hand as energy transfers from skin to ice.
Water warms on a stove but remains water.
Bread browns in a toaster as energy causes chemical changes in the food.
A cold metal spoon warms when placed in hot soup.
questions.explanation: Cooking can involve chemical changes driven by energy input.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of this concept?
choices: Matter changes are often linked to energy transfers.
Energy transfer is easiest to measure when a chemical reaction gives off light.
Physical changes involve matter, while chemical changes involve energy instead.
Energy transfer can be studied without identifying the system or surroundings.
questions.explanation: Physical and chemical changes can both involve energy moving between objects or systems.

questions.prompt: Why does water on a stove not boil instantly at room temperature?
choices: Energy must be transferred to the water to increase its temperature and support the change of state.
Energy must first separate water into hydrogen and oxygen before bubbles appear.
The burner supplies activation energy because boiling is a chemical reaction.
Water must release stored energy before its particles can move faster.
questions.explanation: Boiling requires energy input to heat the water and support the phase change.

questions.prompt: Which situation best shows that a reaction may need energy to begin?
choices: A mixture is stirred so its particles become evenly distributed.
A propane torch needs a spark before the fuel starts burning.
A warm beaker is placed on a balance before a reaction starts.
A solution is measured twice so its mass can be compared.
questions.explanation: The spark provides initial energy so some reactant particles can begin reacting.

questions.prompt: A student says, 'If a reaction releases energy, it should never need energy to start.' What is the misconception?
choices: The student is confusing overall energy release with the activation energy needed at the start.
The student is treating released energy as evidence that matter was created.
The student is assuming activation energy is the energy stored in the products.
The student is mixing up chemical energy with the mass of the reactants.
questions.explanation: Initial energy and overall energy release are different ideas.

questions.prompt: What is activation energy?
choices: The energy released when new product bonds form.
The minimum energy reactants need for a reaction to occur.
The difference in mass between reactants and products.
The heat stored in the container before chemicals are mixed.
questions.explanation: Activation energy is the starting energy barrier for a chemical reaction.

questions.prompt: Why is activation energy often compared to a hill?
choices: It shows that reactants need enough energy to get over a barrier before products can form.
It shows that products must be higher in mass than reactants after a reaction.
It shows that reactants move uphill in the container before they collide.
It shows that gravity supplies the energy needed for chemical reactions.
questions.explanation: The hill model represents the energy barrier that must be overcome.

questions.prompt: If reactant particles collide but do not have enough energy to overcome activation energy, what is most likely?
choices: They will become products more slowly because the container absorbs the missing energy.
They will not react successfully in that collision.
They will form an unstable product that still counts as reactants.
They will transfer heat to the container and then react later.
questions.explanation: A collision must have sufficient energy for the reaction to start.

questions.prompt: Why do reactant particles need to collide for many reactions to occur?
choices: Collisions allow particles to interact and begin bond changes if enough energy is available.
Collisions let reactants share the same container, which is enough for products to form.
Collisions transfer energy to the surroundings before the reaction can start.
Collisions make activation energy unnecessary when particles are close together.
questions.explanation: Reacting particles must interact directly with enough energy to start rearranging bonds.

questions.prompt: Which collision is most likely to lead to a reaction?
choices: One with enough energy and a suitable orientation for bonds to begin changing.
One where particles touch gently and stay close for a long time.
One where particles collide with high energy but in a poor orientation.
One where particles hit the container wall and transfer energy to the glass.
questions.explanation: Successful collisions depend on sufficient energy and an arrangement that can lead to bond changes.

questions.prompt: How does the propane torch example relate to collisions?
choices: A spark mainly separates propane from oxygen so the gases can mix later.
Propane and oxygen particles collide, but the spark lowers the need for energy.
The spark changes propane into oxygen before collisions can happen.
A spark gives some propane and oxygen particles enough energy for successful collisions.
questions.explanation: The spark helps enough reactant particles overcome the activation energy barrier.

kp-230.title: KP5: Initial energy and self-sustaining reactions

questions.prompt: Why can a propane flame continue after the first spark?
choices: The reaction releases enough energy to help more reactant particles overcome activation energy.
The hot products provide oxygen for later collisions in the flame.
The first spark stays in the flame and keeps adding activation energy.
The reaction changes propane into a fuel that reacts with less energy.
questions.explanation: Energy released by early reactions can help sustain later reactions.

questions.prompt: Which statement best distinguishes initial energy input from ongoing reaction energy?
choices: Initial energy comes from the reactants, while ongoing energy comes from the container.
Initial energy is needed because released energy mostly stays in the first products.
Initial energy starts some particles reacting; released energy may keep the reaction going.
Initial energy is used for physical changes, while released energy is used for chemical changes.
questions.explanation: Some reactions need a start but then produce enough energy to continue.

questions.prompt: A fuel-air mixture will not ignite until a spark is added. What does the spark provide?
choices: Enough starting energy for some collisions to be successful.
A small amount of product so the reaction can copy itself.
A boundary that keeps the fuel and air in a closed system.
A source of oxygen that replaces the oxygen already in the air.
questions.explanation: The spark supplies the activation energy needed to begin the reaction.

questions.prompt: When an ice cube melts in your hand, what energy transfer is occurring?
choices: Energy stored in the ice moves into your hand as the ice melts.
The ice releases energy to your hand because the ice feels cold.
Energy moves from your warmer hand to the colder ice.
Melting happens mainly because pressure from your hand changes the ice.
questions.explanation: Physical changes such as melting still involve energy transfer.

questions.prompt: Why is melting ice not evidence by itself that a new substance formed?
choices: The substance remains water, but its physical state changes.
The water molecules rearrange into a new compound as they melt.
The ice reacts with heat to make a gas dissolved in the liquid.
The atoms are regrouped into a different substance during melting.
questions.explanation: Melting changes state without changing chemical identity.

questions.prompt: Which observation would support that your hand loses energy while holding ice?
choices: The ice changes state while your hand stays at the same temperature.
The ice becomes heavier because it absorbs energy from your hand.
The room supplies the energy, so your hand is not part of the transfer.
Your hand feels colder as the ice melts.
questions.explanation: Your hand feels colder because energy is transferred from your hand to the ice.

kp-232.title: KP1: Energy changes in chemical reactions

questions.prompt: Which statement best describes energy in chemical reactions?
choices: Visible flames are the best way to decide whether a reaction involves energy.
Chemical reactions involve energy changes because bonds are broken and formed.
Chemical reactions keep energy inside the reacting materials unless light is produced.
Energy changes matter most in physical changes because chemicals have fixed energy.
questions.explanation: Bond breaking and bond forming mean chemical reactions involve energy changes.

questions.prompt: How are photosynthesis and cellular respiration related to energy?
choices: Both processes destroy energy so organisms can use matter.
Photosynthesis releases energy as heat, while respiration does not involve stored energy.
Photosynthesis stores energy in chemical bonds, while cellular respiration releases stored energy.
Neither process involves chemical reactions.
questions.explanation: The textbook connects these life processes to energy stored and released in chemical bonds.

questions.prompt: A reaction mixture cools down during a reaction. What does this show?
choices: The reaction released heat too quickly for the thermometer to measure.
A colder mixture rules out bond changes in the reacting materials.
Energy transfer occurred between the reaction system and surroundings.
The products have lower temperature because they have less mass.
questions.explanation: Temperature change is evidence that energy moved between system and surroundings.

questions.prompt: In a test tube reaction, what is usually defined as the system?
choices: The classroom air around the test tube
The student's hand touching the tube
Everything except the reacting chemicals
The reacting materials inside the test tube
questions.explanation: Chemists define the system as the materials involved in the reaction.

questions.prompt: Which item is part of the surroundings for a reaction in a flask?
choices: The reactant molecules before they collide.
The flask, nearby air, and anything outside the reacting materials.
The products after they form inside the reaction mixture.
The bonds being broken inside the reactants.
questions.explanation: Surroundings include the container, air, and everything outside the reacting system.

questions.prompt: Why is identifying the system and surroundings useful?
choices: It helps decide whether the reaction mixture or nearby materials are being tracked.
It shows that containers are separate from the reaction and do not exchange energy.
It helps trace whether energy is entering or leaving the reacting materials.
It makes temperature observations less important than naming the reactants.
questions.explanation: Energy can leave or enter the system through the surroundings.

questions.prompt: What does conservation of energy mean in a chemical reaction?
choices: Energy is transferred between system and surroundings, not created or destroyed.
Energy leaves the reaction system and is no longer part of the surroundings.
Bond breaking supplies new energy that was not present before the reaction.
Matter can move between places, but energy stays inside the system.
questions.explanation: The total energy remains constant, but it can move between system and surroundings.

questions.prompt: If an exothermic reaction releases 75 kJ to the surroundings, what happened to the system's energy overall?
choices: The system gained 75 kJ because the surroundings became warmer.
The surroundings lost 75 kJ to the system.
The energy was used up when new bonds formed.
The system lost 75 kJ of energy to the surroundings.
questions.explanation: Energy leaving the system enters the surroundings.

questions.prompt: If a reaction absorbs energy from its surroundings, what should happen to the surroundings if no other energy source replaces it?
choices: The surroundings should warm because absorption adds energy nearby.
The surroundings lose energy and may cool down.
The system and surroundings both gain energy during the same transfer.
The system loses energy to the surroundings as products form.
questions.explanation: Endothermic processes take energy from the surroundings.

questions.prompt: What is an exothermic reaction?
choices: A reaction with a net absorption of energy from the surroundings.
A reaction where no bonds change.
A reaction with a net release of energy to the surroundings.
A reaction where the surroundings stay at the starting temperature.
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions transfer energy out of the chemical system.

questions.prompt: A solution's temperature rises from 21.0°C to 29.5°C during a reaction. What is the temperature change and likely reaction type?
choices: \(+8.5^\circ C\), exothermic
\(-8.5^\circ C\), endothermic
\(+50.5^\circ C\), endothermic
\(0^\circ C\), no energy transfer
questions.explanation: The temperature increased by 8.5°C, suggesting energy was released to the surroundings.

questions.prompt: In an exothermic reaction, how do bond energy changes compare overall?
choices: Breaking old bonds absorbs more energy than forming new bonds releases.
No energy is involved in bonds.
Subscripts release energy while coefficients absorb it.
Forming new bonds releases more energy than breaking old bonds absorbs.
questions.explanation: A net release occurs when bond formation releases more energy than bond breaking absorbs.

questions.prompt: What is an endothermic reaction?
choices: A reaction with a net release of energy to the surroundings.
A reaction with a net absorption of energy from the surroundings.
A reaction where temperature is not useful evidence.
A reaction where products have no energy.
questions.explanation: Endothermic reactions take in energy overall.

questions.prompt: A beaker cools from 24.0°C to 18.5°C during a reaction. What does this suggest?
choices: The reaction is likely exothermic because it warmed the surroundings.
The temperature drop mainly shows that the thermometer absorbed the energy.
The reaction is likely endothermic because it absorbed energy from the surroundings.
The products have less thermal energy because fewer bonds formed.
questions.explanation: The surroundings cooled by 5.5°C, consistent with energy being absorbed by the reaction.

questions.prompt: For an endothermic reaction, what is true about the relative energy of products and reactants in an energy-level diagram?
choices: Products are at a higher energy level than reactants.
Products are lower in energy because heat left the reaction mixture.
Reactants and products must have zero energy.
Energy levels are shown by formula subscripts rather than diagram height.
questions.explanation: Endothermic reactions absorb energy overall, so products are higher in energy.

questions.prompt: Which statement correctly describes bond energy changes?
choices: Breaking bonds releases energy; forming bonds absorbs energy.
Both breaking and forming bonds remove energy from the reaction.
Bonds change without any energy transfer.
Breaking bonds absorbs energy; forming bonds releases energy.
questions.explanation: This comparison is central to explaining endothermic and exothermic reactions.

questions.prompt: A reaction absorbs 180 kJ breaking bonds and releases 240 kJ forming bonds. What is the overall energy change?
choices: 60 kJ absorbed overall
60 kJ released overall
420 kJ absorbed overall
No energy change
questions.explanation: 240 kJ released minus 180 kJ absorbed gives 60 kJ released overall.

questions.prompt: A reaction absorbs more energy breaking bonds than it releases forming bonds. How should it be classified?
choices: Exothermic
Balanced but not chemical
Endothermic
A closed-system measurement
questions.explanation: If the system absorbs more energy than it releases, the reaction is endothermic overall.

questions.prompt: Why can temperature change be useful evidence during a reaction?
choices: It can show whether energy moved into or out of the surroundings.
It identifies the products more directly than colour or gas observations.
It shows whether the reaction changed the number of atoms present.
It is useful even if the system and surroundings are not defined.
questions.explanation: Temperature change is a measurable sign of energy transfer.

questions.prompt: A reaction mixture gets warmer in a test tube. What is the most likely energy interpretation?
choices: Energy was absorbed from the surroundings into the system.
No bonds were formed because the tube got warmer.
The reaction must be photosynthesis.
Energy was released from the reaction system to the surroundings.
questions.explanation: A warmer surrounding solution or container suggests an exothermic change.

questions.prompt: A student wants stronger evidence than 'it felt cold.' What data should they collect?
choices: A drawing of the test tube shape.
Temperature measurements over time before and after mixing reactants.
The names of the students nearby.
The colour of the thermometer casing.
questions.explanation: Quantitative temperature data makes the energy-transfer claim stronger.

questions.prompt: In an energy-level diagram, products are shown lower than reactants. What type of reaction does this represent?
choices: Endothermic
No energy transfer
Exothermic
An open-system measurement
questions.explanation: Lower-energy products indicate energy was released overall.

questions.prompt: What does the vertical difference between reactants and products represent in an energy-level diagram?
choices: The overall energy change of the reaction
The amount of activation energy still stored in the container.
The relative mass difference between reactants and products.
The temperature reading that must be measured with a thermometer.
questions.explanation: Energy-level diagrams compare relative energies to show net absorption or release.

questions.prompt: A diagram shows products above reactants and an arrow labelled energy absorbed. What is the correct interpretation?
choices: The reaction is exothermic because products are above reactants.
The reaction involves energy but no bond changes.
The products have less energy than reactants.
The reaction is endothermic because the products have higher energy.
questions.explanation: Higher-energy products match an overall absorption of energy.

questions.prompt: Why can luminol help forensic investigators find possible blood evidence?
choices: It makes possible blood traces visible by changing the fabric colour permanently.
It can react with iron from hemoglobin and emit a blue-green glow in the dark.
It separates blood from other stains so investigators can collect it directly.
It confirms the identity of the person before DNA testing is needed.
questions.explanation: The glow can reveal traces that may contain blood, but it is a screening test.

questions.prompt: Why is a positive luminol test not conclusive proof that blood is present?
choices: Luminol tests for DNA rather than for a chemical reaction that produces light.
The glow means iron is present, so the source of the iron still matters.
Other substances can also cause luminol to emit light.
A positive test shows a stain is biological but not which body fluid it is.
questions.explanation: Some materials containing certain metals or other chemicals can also trigger luminol.

questions.prompt: What energy change causes the visible glow in a luminol reaction?
choices: Energy is released as light when excited electrons return to lower energy levels.
Energy is absorbed as light when electrons move back to higher energy levels.
Hemoglobin stores the blue-green colour until luminol releases it.
Electrons gain mass during the reaction and the added mass appears as light.
questions.explanation: The emitted light comes from electrons releasing energy as they drop back down.

questions.prompt: What happens to an electron in luminol when it becomes excited?
choices: It releases light first and then moves to a higher energy level.
It gains energy but stays at the same energy level.
It absorbs energy and moves to a higher energy level.
It gives up energy before moving to a higher level.
questions.explanation: An excited electron has absorbed energy and moved to a higher energy level.

questions.prompt: Why does the luminol reaction produce visible light?
choices: Excited electrons release energy as light when they return to lower energy levels.
Luminol reflects light from the room more strongly after it is sprayed.
Iron atoms make luminol glow without an electron energy change.
The solution reflects room light after the investigator darkens the room.
questions.explanation: The glow is caused by energy released as electrons drop back down.

questions.prompt: Which sequence best describes the light-producing energy change?
choices: Electron returns to a lower level -> energy absorbed -> light stored
Energy absorbed -> electron higher level -> energy released mainly as heat
Lower energy level -> electron absorbs light -> no emission possible
Energy absorbed by electrons -> higher energy level -> return to lower level -> light emitted
questions.explanation: The order connects electron excitation with later light emission.

questions.prompt: Why should investigators not treat a positive luminol test as final proof of blood?
choices: A strong glow means the stain has already been identified as blood.
Some non-blood substances can also make luminol glow.
The test is less useful when the stain is not visible before spraying.
The glow shows the stain is recent enough to identify a person.
questions.explanation: Luminol is useful for screening, but it can have false positives.

questions.prompt: After a luminol glow appears on fabric, what is a reasonable next step?
choices: Identify the source from the colour of the glow alone.
Ignore the result because a screening test is not final proof.
Use further tests, such as blood confirmation or DNA analysis, before drawing a strong conclusion.
Repeat the luminol spray until the glow confirms the exact material.
questions.explanation: Further testing can distinguish possible blood evidence from other luminol-reactive materials.

questions.prompt: Which statement best describes the strength of luminol evidence?
choices: It can point investigators toward possible evidence, but it needs confirmation.
It is strong enough to replace blood confirmation tests.
It identifies the source of a stain if the glow is bright enough.
It is most reliable when investigators skip later DNA testing.
questions.explanation: Luminol is sensitive and useful, but positive results have limits.

questions.prompt: What does reaction rate describe?
choices: The total energy change between reactants and products.
The temperature at which products become stable.
Whether reactants are written before products in an equation.
How quickly reactants are changed into products under specific conditions.
questions.explanation: Reaction rate is about speed of chemical change.

questions.prompt: Why might it be useful for a reaction such as rusting to occur slowly rather than quickly?
choices: Slow reactions are easier to reverse because fewer bonds change.
A slower rate can make damage easier to manage or prevent over time.
Slow reactions produce different atoms than fast reactions of the same materials.
Slow reactions are usually physical changes rather than chemical changes.
questions.explanation: Whether a rate is desirable depends on the context and purpose.

questions.prompt: Which situation most clearly depends on controlling reaction rate?
choices: Choosing a larger beaker so reactants have more space.
Writing the product names before the reactant names.
Preventing an industrial reaction from heating up too quickly.
Deciding whether a reaction is open or closed after it finishes.
questions.explanation: Industrial safety often depends on keeping reaction rates under control.

questions.prompt: Which change would usually speed up a reaction by increasing particle motion?
choices: Increasing temperature
Lowering reactant concentration so particles collide less often
Using larger pieces so less surface is exposed
Reducing particle motion by cooling the mixture
questions.explanation: Higher temperature often increases collision frequency and collision energy.

questions.prompt: Why can powdered magnesium react faster than a solid strip of the same mass?
choices: Powdered magnesium has less mass in each small piece, so atoms react differently.
The strip contains the same element but fewer exposed collision sites.
Surface area matters mainly because it changes the colour of the metal.
The powder has more exposed surface area for collisions.
questions.explanation: More surface area gives reactant particles more places to collide.

questions.prompt: Which list contains factors that can affect reaction rate?
choices: Colour, container shape, and the order of product names.
Temperature, surface area, reactant concentration or amount, and catalysts.
Mass conservation, product colour, and the balance brand.
Whether the reaction is written as a word equation or a formula equation.
questions.explanation: These factors can change the number or success of reactant collisions.

questions.prompt: What is a catalyst?
choices: A reactant that is added in small amounts and used up first.
A substance that changes the products but does not affect rate.
A substance that speeds up a reaction without being used up overall.
A material that stores heat so collisions are no longer needed.
questions.explanation: Catalysts increase reaction rate and are not consumed overall.

questions.prompt: How does a catalytic converter connect to reaction rate?
choices: It uses catalyst materials to speed reactions that convert harmful exhaust gases into less harmful substances.
It slows exhaust reactions so gases have more time to cool before leaving the vehicle.
It adds fuel vapour so exhaust gases can finish burning in the converter.
It traps exhaust gases without changing their chemical composition.
questions.explanation: Catalytic converters are real applications of catalysts.

questions.prompt: Why are enzymes described as catalysts in body cells?
choices: They are changed into products each time they help a reaction.
They make useful reactions occur mainly by raising body temperature.
They supply extra reactant atoms for the products being formed.
They help important reactions occur fast enough without being used up in each reaction.
questions.explanation: Enzymes speed up biological reactions under conditions compatible with life.

questions.prompt: What can cause a runaway reaction in a large industrial vessel?
choices: The reaction releases heat that is removed faster than reactants can collide.
Heat released by an exothermic reaction increases reaction rate, releasing even more heat.
The container absorbs the products, so the reaction rate is measured incorrectly.
Products form with less energy, so the reaction stops transferring heat.
questions.explanation: Heat can make a reaction self-accelerate if it is not removed safely.

questions.prompt: Why is scaling up a lab reaction to industry risky?
choices: Large vessels make reactant collisions rare, so the reaction type changes.
Industrial containers make exothermic reactions absorb energy overall.
Large amounts of reacting material can release heat faster than it is removed.
Scaling up reduces the need to monitor temperature if the same chemicals are used.
questions.explanation: Heat management becomes more important at larger scale.

questions.prompt: Which safety control directly addresses the risk of runaway reactions?
choices: Designing systems to remove excess heat and monitor temperature
Adding reactants faster so the reaction finishes before heat builds up.
Sealing heat inside the vessel so the surroundings stay protected.
Choosing a larger vessel without changing heat-removal design.
questions.explanation: Monitoring and heat removal help keep reaction rate under control.

questions.prompt: In shape-shifting paper, what happens when thermoplastic polymers absorb energy in an oven?
choices: The polymer chains cool and lock into the stretched shape.
The paper dries out, so ordinary folds hold their position.
The polymer absorbs heat but the cuts in the paper do most of the shrinking.
Molecular motion increases and the polymer can shrink from its stretched shape.
questions.explanation: Energy input lets the polymer chains move out of their forced elongated shape.

questions.prompt: How does polymer shrinkage help the paper fold?
choices: Shrinking makes the whole sheet expand evenly while staying flat.
Shrinking in selected places lifts and bends the paper into a three-dimensional form.
The polymer dissolves from the paper and leaves gaps that pull it inward.
Heating weakens the paper fibres, so gravity determines the final shape.
questions.explanation: Placed polymer strips can pull parts of the paper as they shrink.

questions.prompt: Which idea connects this material to energy transfer?
choices: Energy transfer matters mainly because it changes the printed fold pattern.
The oven supplies heat, which becomes extra matter in the polymer.
Energy absorbed by the polymer changes molecular motion and material shape.
The polymer stores heat without changing its molecular arrangement.
questions.explanation: The material's behaviour depends on energy input changing particle motion.

questions.prompt: Why is shape-shifting paper an example of chemistry supporting material design?
choices: The chemical and physical properties of polymers are used to create useful structures.
The design relies on cutting patterns, while polymer behaviour mainly changes appearance.
The paper folds because heating changes the external air pressure around it.
The polymer is chosen because it resists molecular motion in the oven.
questions.explanation: Material design can use molecular behaviour to produce large-scale structure.

questions.prompt: Which design goal could be supported by lightweight shape-shifting materials?
choices: Making packaging thicker so strength comes mostly from extra mass.
Choosing materials that soften but do not hold a useful shape.
Using a drawing to plan folds without considering material response.
Creating packaging that forms a strong structure with less material.
questions.explanation: Light, strong, lower-material structures can be useful design targets.

questions.prompt: A designer chooses a polymer because it shrinks predictably when heated. What chemistry idea are they using?
choices: A polymer formula can be rearranged on paper to make the sheet fold.
Material properties can be matched to a desired function.
A catalyst is needed because this shape change is treated as a chemical reaction.
Heating changes the design drawing rather than the material.
questions.explanation: Design can rely on predictable material responses to conditions.

questions.prompt: What does the shape-shifting paper example show about science and creativity?
choices: Design ideas are creative, but chemistry mainly matters after a product is finished.
Creative ideas are strongest when testing is replaced by appearance.
Ideas from chemistry, art, design, and engineering can combine to create new technologies.
Engineering uses shape and structure, while chemistry mostly supplies names for materials.
questions.explanation: Innovation often comes from combining knowledge across fields.

questions.prompt: Which project best connects a personal interest with science?
choices: Using polymer behaviour to design an origami-inspired package
Choosing an origami pattern first and assuming the material will respond as planned
Making a toy attractive before checking whether heat changes the material safely
Selecting a bright material because appearance is easier to evaluate than performance
questions.explanation: A strong project uses science concepts to solve a creative design problem.

questions.prompt: Why should creative science designs still be evaluated?
choices: Evaluation is mainly for advertising, because a creative design has already shown its purpose.
Safety checks matter less when a material is lightweight.
A design that folds correctly once should perform similarly in different conditions.
They must be checked for function, safety, cost, and environmental impact.
questions.explanation: Good innovation balances creativity with evidence and responsibility.

questions.prompt: Which career task clearly uses chemistry knowledge?
choices: A technician sorts sample labels but does not interpret the materials.
A forensic scientist analyzes chemical evidence from a scene.
A worker records equipment locations before samples are tested.
A planner schedules lab staff without interpreting chemical results.
questions.explanation: Forensic science can depend on chemical tests and evidence interpretation.

questions.prompt: Why might a mudlogger need chemistry and geology knowledge?
choices: They mainly use chemistry to choose which drilling tools are easiest to label.
They record underground sample depth but leave composition unexplained.
They analyze underground rock samples and information related to oil and gas deposits.
They focus on surface conditions because rock chemistry is unrelated to deposits.
questions.explanation: The textbook connects mudlogging to chemistry, geology, and underground samples.

questions.prompt: Which pair of careers from this section both connect to chemical processes or materials?
choices: Food scientist and materials technician
Architectural drafter and lab schedule coordinator
Graphic designer and equipment inventory clerk
Tour guide and field-trip photographer
questions.explanation: Food science and materials work can both require chemical knowledge.

questions.prompt: How can a fire protection engineer use chemistry?
choices: By focusing on alarm placement while treating material fire risk as mostly similar.
By choosing suppression systems mainly from their cost and appearance.
By understanding combustion, suppression chemicals, material risks, and emergency planning.
By assuming ventilation design matters more than the chemistry of combustion.
questions.explanation: Fire protection involves chemical processes, materials, design, and safety.

questions.prompt: Which public-safety decision depends on chemistry?
choices: Choosing a response plan for a spill based on the chemical's hazards
Choosing a storage room based mainly on how close it is to the entrance
Counting containers before reading the safety information
Using the same cleanup method for liquids that look similar
questions.explanation: Chemical hazards influence safe handling, response, and protection.

questions.prompt: Why do environmental assessors need chemistry knowledge?
choices: They estimate risk mainly from where a sample was taken, not what it contains.
They treat dilution as a complete solution once a pollutant is hard to see.
They focus on property boundaries before considering how substances move.
They may evaluate how substances move through and affect soil, water, air, and living systems.
questions.explanation: Environmental protection often requires understanding chemical behaviour and risk.

questions.prompt: What might a fuel researcher compare when studying fuels?
choices: Container labels and colour, because those summarize fuel quality.
Energy output, emissions, renewability, and environmental impacts.
Fuel names and marketing claims before checking reaction evidence.
Whether a fuel burns quickly, without considering by-products.
questions.explanation: Fuel research connects chemistry to performance and sustainability.

questions.prompt: Why are alternative fuels part of a chemistry-and-sustainability discussion?
choices: Alternative fuels are mainly a transportation issue, not a chemical one.
Fuel choices matter mostly for cost, while emissions are separate from chemistry.
Different reactions and fuel sources can have different emissions and impacts.
A renewable source makes reaction products less important to measure.
questions.explanation: Energy decisions depend on chemical processes and their consequences.

questions.prompt: Which question would best guide a sustainable fuel investigation?
choices: What are the useful energy output, by-products, costs, and environmental trade-offs?
Which fuel has the simplest formula to write in a notebook?
Can the fuel burn hot enough to make by-products less important?
Which fuel is easiest to transport before checking emissions or renewability?
questions.explanation: Sustainability requires evaluating benefits and impacts together.

kp-253.title: KP1: Temperature data and reaction type

questions.prompt: A reaction mixture changes from 19.8°C to 34.6°C. What does this temperature data suggest?
choices: The reaction is endothermic because the surroundings warmed.
No energy transfer occurred because the reaction still has products.
The mixture warmed because the thermometer supplied energy to the reaction.
The reaction is exothermic because the surroundings warmed.
questions.explanation: A temperature increase in the reaction mixture suggests energy was released to the surroundings.

questions.prompt: A reaction mixture changes from 22.4°C to 16.9°C. What is the temperature change?
choices: \(0^\circ C\)
questions.explanation: Final minus initial is 16.9 - 22.4 = -5.5°C.

questions.prompt: Why should temperature be recorded over time during an energy investigation?
choices: It balances the chemical equation automatically.
It identifies the products even if no other observations are recorded.
It shows the direction and size of the temperature change as the reaction proceeds.
It replaces the need to record colour, gas, or solid formation.
questions.explanation: Temperature-time data supports a claim about energy transfer.

questions.prompt: A reaction absorbs 95 kJ to break bonds and releases 130 kJ when new bonds form. What is the net energy change?
choices: 35 kJ released overall
35 kJ absorbed overall
225 kJ absorbed overall
No overall change
questions.explanation: 130 kJ released minus 95 kJ absorbed gives 35 kJ released overall.

questions.prompt: Which data would best support a claim that a reaction was endothermic?
choices: Temperature increased and heat was released.
No temperature measurements were made.
The reactants felt cool before mixing, but no change was measured.
Temperature decreased while the reaction system absorbed energy from surroundings.
questions.explanation: Endothermic reactions absorb energy overall and often cool the surroundings.

questions.prompt: Why is overall energy change called a net result?
choices: It is based mainly on the colour of the reactants.
It compares energy absorbed in bond breaking with energy released in bond forming.
It describes bond breaking but leaves out energy released by new bonds.
It counts atoms conserved in the equation but does not compare bond energies.
questions.explanation: The overall change depends on the balance of absorbed and released energy.

questions.prompt: Which set of observations would best support that a chemical reaction occurred?
choices: The solution was stirred in the same beaker as before.
The lab sheet had the date and reactant names recorded.
Temperature change, gas production, colour change, or formation of a new solid.
The beaker was rinsed before the reactants were mixed.
questions.explanation: These observations can indicate new substances and energy transfer.

questions.prompt: In an aluminum and copper(II) chloride investigation, the solution colour changes and a solid forms. What is a reasonable interpretation?
choices: New substances likely formed as atoms and ions rearranged.
The colour change is probably from the thermometer touching the solution.
The atoms changed size but kept the same bonding arrangement.
The solid was likely a piece of filter paper breaking apart.
questions.explanation: Colour change and solid formation can be evidence of chemical change.

questions.prompt: Why should observations be recorded during a reaction, not only at the end?
choices: Final observations are enough because the products contain the whole reaction history.
Recording observations is useful mainly for drawing the apparatus later.
The reaction name can suggest what happened, but evidence is still needed.
Changes over time help connect evidence to the process of the reaction.
questions.explanation: The timing and sequence of observations can support explanations.

questions.prompt: What should a particle-level explanation of a chemical reaction include?
choices: Whether the solution looked more interesting after mixing.
Atoms, ions, bonds broken, bonds formed, and how particles rearrange.
The brand of glassware and the order of cleanup steps.
The colour changes, without describing particles or bonds.
questions.explanation: Particle-level explanations connect observations to atoms, ions, and bonds.

questions.prompt: Why is 'the test tube got warm' not a complete particle-level explanation?
choices: It gives an energy clue but not a particle model.
It gives the final observation but leaves out which bonds changed.
It describes evidence but not what happened to atoms, ions, and bonds.
It is useful mainly because temperature observations are easier than particle models.
questions.explanation: Temperature is evidence; a deeper explanation describes particles and energy changes.

questions.prompt: Which statement is most particle-level?
choices: Aluminum atoms become ions while copper ions form copper atoms, so particles rearrange into new substances.
The solution changed colour and the beaker felt warmer.
The reaction made a visible solid after several minutes.
The mixture was filtered after the reaction slowed down.
questions.explanation: This explanation identifies particle changes instead of only visible observations.

questions.prompt: Why can dissolving an ionic compound in water be useful for a hand warmer investigation?
choices: Dissolving separates particles but does not affect surrounding temperature.
Dissolving is useful mainly when a gas is produced.
Water absorbs the compound without interacting with its ions.
Some dissolving processes release energy and raise the temperature.
questions.explanation: Some ionic compounds dissolve exothermically, making them possible heat sources.

questions.prompt: A salt dissolves in water and the temperature drops from 20.0°C to 13.0°C. What type of energy change is suggested?
choices: Exothermic dissolving
Endothermic dissolving
No energy transfer
Combustion
questions.explanation: A temperature decrease suggests the dissolving process absorbed energy from surroundings.

questions.prompt: Which data would help decide whether a substance could work in a hand warmer?
choices: The colour and texture of the dry solid before water is added.
The label information without measuring any temperature change.
Temperature change after dissolving a measured amount in water.
The time it takes to open the packet before mixing begins.
questions.explanation: Temperature data shows whether dissolving releases enough useful heat.

questions.prompt: Why should the 'best' hand warmer not be chosen only by the highest temperature?
choices: It must also be safe, practical, affordable, and environmentally responsible.
Temperature matters, but a single high reading does not describe the full design problem.
The hottest chemical is the best choice if it is sealed inside a packet.
Environmental impact can be checked after the product is sold.
questions.explanation: Good design balances performance with constraints and safety.

questions.prompt: Which criterion directly protects the user of a hand warmer?
choices: A compound name that is easy for users to pronounce.
A package that looks durable before temperature testing.
A large packet volume so the product feels substantial.
Maximum temperature that will not harm skin.
questions.explanation: A hand warmer must produce useful warmth without burning the user.

questions.prompt: A chemical warms water quickly but is expensive and difficult to dispose of safely. What should a design team do?
choices: Choose it automatically because it gets hot.
Compare it against the full set of criteria before deciding whether it is suitable.
Use it if the first temperature reading is higher than room temperature.
Delay safety and disposal decisions until after packaging is designed.
questions.explanation: Design decisions should weigh multiple criteria, not just one measurement.

questions.prompt: Which safety factor should be included in designing a chemical hand warmer?
choices: Whether the package colour is the user's favourite.
Whether the chemical name looks familiar to users.
Whether the reactants or products can irritate skin or harm the environment.
Whether the packet feels warm before any chemical testing.
questions.explanation: Chemical products must be evaluated for safe use, cleanup, and disposal.

questions.prompt: Why should cleanup and disposal instructions be part of a chemical product design?
choices: The materials still exist after use and may affect people or the environment.
Used chemicals are less important once the heat has been released.
Disposal is mainly a packaging issue, separate from chemical behaviour.
Instructions improve user confidence but do not affect safe handling.
questions.explanation: Conservation of matter and chemical risk both matter after the product is used.

questions.prompt: Which design choice best reflects safe chemical design?
choices: Letting users touch unknown powders directly.
Choosing the most reactive chemical without testing.
Using a smaller label and relying on users to infer the risks.
Using a sealed packet, clear handling instructions, and tested disposal procedures.
questions.explanation: Safe design anticipates handling, containment, and end-of-use risks.

kp-260.title: KP1: Chemical reactions as energy sources

questions.prompt: Which example uses a chemical reaction as an energy source?
choices: Using stored spring energy to open a clip.
Burning propane to heat food.
Warming soup by placing it in sunlight.
Moving a fan with electricity already supplied from a wall outlet.
questions.explanation: Combustion of propane releases useful energy for heating and cooking.

questions.prompt: Why should an energy-source investigation include both pros and cons?
choices: A fuel's name usually tells whether its impacts are acceptable.
Energy sources with high output tend to have similar impacts.
Useful energy output, emissions, cost, and environmental impacts each matter.
Balanced equations are enough to answer most social and environmental questions.
questions.explanation: Energy choices involve chemical, environmental, and socio-economic trade-offs.

questions.prompt: Which comparison fits this investigation topic?
choices: Fossil-fuel combustion and hydrogen fuel cells both involve chemical reactions, but they can differ in products and impacts.
Hydrogen fuel cells are mainly electrical devices, so their chemical products are not important.
Gasoline releases energy mostly because it evaporates quickly.
Alternative energy sources should be compared by output, not by reaction products.
questions.explanation: Different energy technologies can use different reactions with different outcomes.

kp-261.title: KP2: Balanced equations and energy use

questions.prompt: Why should an energy-source investigation include a balanced chemical equation?
choices: It shows that a reaction has products, so the investigation does not need particle models.
It shows energy output directly from the size of the coefficients.
It shows the atoms conserved and the reactant-product ratios involved in the energy-producing reaction.
It identifies the fuel source but not the rearrangement of atoms during the reaction.
questions.explanation: A balanced equation connects the reaction to atom rearrangement, bond changes, and fuel use.

questions.prompt: Which equation is balanced for hydrogen reacting with oxygen to produce water?
questions.explanation: The balanced equation has 4 H atoms and 2 O atoms on both sides.

questions.prompt: How does bond information help explain useful energy from a fuel reaction?
choices: Bond changes matter mainly when the fuel is a solid rather than a gas.
Coefficients show amounts, but they do not show bond energies by themselves.
Energy output comes from products having less mass than the reactants.
Energy is absorbed when bonds break and released when new bonds form, so the net change matters.
questions.explanation: Useful energy depends on the overall balance of bond-breaking and bond-forming energy.

questions.prompt: Which evaluation compares both advantages and disadvantages of gasoline as an energy source?
choices: It is useful for transportation, so the evaluation should focus mainly on engine performance.
It stores useful energy for transportation, but combustion products and extraction impacts must be considered.
Its emissions make the chemistry less important than the social effects of transportation.
Its chemical equation is balanced, so social impacts do not matter.
questions.explanation: Energy sources should be evaluated using benefits and trade-offs.

questions.prompt: Which factor belongs in a broad evaluation of an energy source?
choices: Whether the reaction arrow points toward products.
Whether the fuel name is familiar to consumers.
Environmental, social, economic, and energy-use impacts.
The colour of the flame as the main evidence of sustainability.
questions.explanation: A broad evaluation includes more than the chemistry alone.

questions.prompt: A hydrogen fuel cell produces useful energy and water as a product, but hydrogen production requires energy. What does this show?
choices: A complete evaluation must include how the fuel is made, used, and supplied.
Hydrogen fuel cells can be evaluated from the product side without considering the fuel source.
The final product is the main factor, so production energy is a separate topic.
Water as a product makes hydrogen production impacts less relevant to the comparison.
questions.explanation: Energy technologies should be assessed over the larger process, not just one reaction step.

questions.prompt: Which description best matches a synthesis reaction?
choices: One compound absorbs energy and separates into simpler substances.
Two aqueous compounds exchange ions and may form a precipitate.
A fuel reacts with oxygen to form oxides and release energy.
Two or more reactants combine to form one compound.
questions.explanation: Synthesis reactions build a single product from simpler reactants.

questions.prompt: Which equation is a synthesis reaction?
choices: \(Zn + CuSO_4 -> ZnSO_4 + Cu\)
\(AgNO_3 + NaCl -> AgCl + NaNO_3\)
questions.explanation: Magnesium and oxygen combine to form one compound, magnesium oxide.

questions.prompt: What feature should you look for when classifying a synthesis reaction?
choices: One reactant separating into several simpler products.
Two compounds trading ions while staying in solution.
Several reactants forming a single product.
A solid forming after two dissolved compounds exchange ions.
questions.explanation: The product side of a synthesis reaction typically has one compound.

questions.prompt: Which pattern represents synthesis?
choices: \(A + BX -> AX + B\)
\(AX + BY -> AY + BX\)
questions.explanation: The synthesis pattern combines parts into one product.

questions.prompt: A metal and a non-metal combine to form an ionic compound. Which classification is most likely?
choices: Double replacement, because ions are involved in the compound
Decomposition, because the product contains more than one element
Neutralization, because ionic compounds can dissolve in water
Synthesis
questions.explanation: Combining simpler reactants into one compound matches synthesis.

questions.prompt: Which balanced equation fits the pattern \(A + B -> AB\)?
choices: \(CaCO_3 -> CaO + CO_2\)
\(Cl_2 + 2NaBr -> 2NaCl + Br_2\)
\(BaCl_2 + Na_2SO_4 -> BaSO_4 + 2NaCl\)
questions.explanation: Potassium and chlorine combine to form potassium chloride.

questions.prompt: Why are many synthesis reactions exothermic?
choices: Synthesis reactions release energy because a single product has fewer formulas to write.
Energy release happens because reactant atoms disappear into the product.
Forming new bonds in the product can release more energy than was needed to start the reaction.
A single product means the total mass has less ability to store energy.
questions.explanation: Many synthesis reactions release energy overall as stable products form.

questions.prompt: A metal reacts with chlorine gas and gives off bright light and heat while forming a salt. What does the energy evidence suggest?
choices: The synthesis reaction is exothermic.
The reaction must be endothermic because a solid product formed.
The light is evidence that atoms changed state but did not rearrange.
The salt absorbed the heat, so energy was not transferred to the surroundings.
questions.explanation: Heat and light are evidence of energy released to the surroundings.

questions.prompt: Which observation best supports energy release during product formation?
choices: The reactants are listed in the same order as the product formula.
The equation has more reactant formulas than product formulas.
The product name includes pieces of both reactant names.
The reaction container becomes warmer as one compound forms.
questions.explanation: A temperature increase is evidence of exothermic energy transfer.

kp-266.title: KP4: Synthesis in the environment and technology

questions.prompt: Which example from environmental chemistry can involve synthesis?
choices: One compound separating into two simpler substances.
Sulfur trioxide combining with water to form sulfuric acid.
Two dissolved salts exchanging ions to make a precipitate.
A metal atom replacing another metal ion in solution.
questions.explanation: SO3 and water combine into one acid compound, matching synthesis.

questions.prompt: How is rust formation related to synthesis reactions?
choices: Iron oxide breaks into iron and oxygen during ordinary rusting.
Rusting is mainly a surface colour change rather than formation of a new compound.
Iron and oxygen combine through reactions that produce iron oxide.
Rust formation rearranges surface particles but does not form a new compound.
questions.explanation: Rusting involves atoms combining into iron oxide products.

questions.prompt: Why can a hydrogen fuel cell reaction be viewed as synthesis?
choices: Hydrogen and oxygen combine to form water.
Water breaks apart into hydrogen and oxygen before electricity is produced.
Two dissolved compounds exchange ions to make water.
Hydrogen replaces oxygen in a compound during the reaction.
questions.explanation: The reaction combines two reactants into water as the chemical product.

questions.prompt: What is a decomposition reaction?
choices: Two elements combine and release energy as one compound forms.
Two aqueous compounds exchange ions to form new compounds.
A compound reacts with oxygen to form oxides and release energy.
One compound breaks down into elements or simpler compounds.
questions.explanation: Decomposition reactions start with one compound and produce simpler substances.

questions.prompt: Which equation is a decomposition reaction?
choices: \(2Na + Cl_2 -> 2NaCl\)
\(Cu + 2AgNO_3 -> Cu(NO_3)_2 + 2Ag\)
questions.explanation: Mercury(II) oxide breaks into mercury and oxygen.

questions.prompt: What feature should you look for to identify decomposition?
choices: Two reactants combining to produce one compound.
Oxygen reacting with a fuel to produce oxides.
One reactant producing two or more products.
An element taking the place of another element in a compound.
questions.explanation: The reactant side usually has one compound in decomposition.

questions.prompt: Which pattern represents decomposition?
choices: \(A + BX -> AX + B\)
questions.explanation: Decomposition breaks one compound into simpler products.

questions.prompt: Why is decomposition often described as the reverse of synthesis?
choices: Both use the same atoms, but decomposition is written with fewer products.
Decomposition makes a single product when the reactant has several elements.
Synthesis changes bonds, while decomposition mainly changes particle spacing.
Synthesis combines parts into one compound, while decomposition splits one compound apart.
questions.explanation: Their general patterns point in opposite directions.

questions.prompt: Which reaction fits \(AB -> A + B\) most closely?
choices: \(Zn + 2HCl -> ZnCl_2 + H_2\)
\(AgNO_3 + KCl -> AgCl + KNO_3\)
questions.explanation: Water is split into hydrogen and oxygen.

questions.prompt: Why do most decomposition reactions require energy input?
choices: Energy input is needed because product formulas are written after the arrow.
The products usually have simpler names than the reactant.
Enough energy must be absorbed to break bonds in the reactant compound.
Decomposition changes state but keeps the bonding pattern the same.
questions.explanation: Breaking bonds requires energy, so decomposition often needs heat, electricity, or light.

questions.prompt: Heating calcium carbonate to produce calcium oxide and carbon dioxide is best classified as what?
choices: An endothermic decomposition reaction
An exothermic synthesis reaction
A double replacement reaction
A combustion reaction
questions.explanation: One compound breaks apart and heat is used to drive the reaction.

questions.prompt: Which energy source could start a decomposition reaction?
choices: Changing a coefficient so fewer atoms need to be rearranged.
Removing products so the compound separates more easily.
Cooling the reactant so its bonds become easier to break.
Heat or electricity supplied to the reactant.
questions.explanation: Energy input can provide the energy needed to break reactant bonds.

questions.prompt: What happens in a single replacement reaction?
choices: Two compounds exchange ions and keep the same elements paired.
An element takes the place of another element in a compound.
One compound splits into simpler products after absorbing energy.
A fuel reacts with oxygen to form oxides and release energy.
questions.explanation: Single replacement involves one free element replacing an element in a compound.

questions.prompt: Which equation is a single replacement reaction?
choices: \(2Na + Cl_2 -> 2NaCl\)
\(CaCO_3 -> CaO + CO_2\)
\(Fe + CuSO_4 -> FeSO_4 + Cu\)
questions.explanation: Iron replaces copper in the copper(II) sulfate compound.

questions.prompt: What reactant pattern is typical for single replacement?
choices: An element plus a compound
Two dissolved ionic compounds that exchange ions
One compound that absorbs energy and breaks apart
A fuel and oxygen reacting to produce oxides
questions.explanation: Single replacement starts with a free element reacting with a compound.

questions.prompt: Which pattern represents a metal replacing a metal ion?
choices: \(A + BX -> AX + B\)
\(AX + BY -> AY + BX\)
questions.explanation: The free metal A becomes part of the compound while B becomes the free element.

questions.prompt: In \(Cu + 2AgNO_3 -> Cu(NO_3)_2 + 2Ag\), what is replaced?
choices: Silver ions are replaced by copper.
Copper ions are replaced by silver atoms.
Nitrate ions are replaced by copper ions.
Silver atoms replace nitrate ions in the compound.
questions.explanation: Copper takes the place of silver in the nitrate compound.

questions.prompt: Why is an aqueous compound often involved in metal replacement reactions?
choices: Water supplies the electrons needed to make a metal product.
Aqueous compounds let the compound split into atoms rather than ions.
Dissolving makes the metal ion disappear before the reaction begins.
Dissolving separates ions so a free metal can interact with metal ions.
questions.explanation: Aqueous solutions allow ions to move and react.

questions.prompt: Which pattern represents a non-metal replacing a non-metal ion?
choices: \(AX + Y -> AY + X\)
\(AX + BY -> AY + BX\)
questions.explanation: The free non-metal Y replaces X in the compound.

questions.prompt: In \(Cl_2 + 2NaBr -> 2NaCl + Br_2\), which element is produced as a free non-metal?
choices: \(NaCl\)
questions.explanation: Chlorine replaces bromide, so bromine is released as Br2.

questions.prompt: What observation could indicate bromine formed in a non-metal replacement reaction?
choices: A new brown colour appears in the solution.
The solution becomes colourless because bromide ions stay unchanged.
A grey metal coating appears on a wire in the solution.
The sodium ions become the main visible product.
questions.explanation: The textbook example connects bromine formation with a brown colour.

kp-273.title: KP4: Hydrogen as a replaceable element

questions.prompt: In some single replacement reactions with water, how can hydrogen behave?
choices: Like an ion that stays paired with hydroxide throughout the reaction.
Like a spectator ion that is not involved in the reaction.
Like oxygen in combustion because it forms an oxide product.
Like a metal that can be replaced by a more reactive metal.
questions.explanation: Hydrogen in water can be replaced, producing hydrogen gas.

questions.prompt: Which product would you expect when lithium reacts with water in a single replacement reaction?
choices: Carbon dioxide gas
Hydrogen gas
Sulfur dioxide gas
Solid silver
questions.explanation: Lithium replaces hydrogen in water, producing H2 gas.

questions.prompt: What evidence might show hydrogen gas is produced when a reactive metal is placed in water?
choices: The water becomes cloudy because a precipitate of hydrogen forms.
A hydrocarbon forms because water contains hydrogen atoms.
Bubbles form at the metal surface.
The metal surface becomes smoother without producing gas.
questions.explanation: Gas production often appears as bubbling.

kp-274.title: KP5: Applications of single replacement reactions

questions.prompt: Why can the reaction between aluminum powder and iron(III) oxide be useful for welding?
choices: It releases enough heat to melt the iron produced.
It absorbs heat from the track, causing iron to solidify between rails.
It produces aluminum metal, which replaces the need for molten iron.
It is a replacement reaction, but the energy release is too small to matter.
questions.explanation: Some single replacement reactions release a large amount of energy.

questions.prompt: Which application involves producing bromine by single replacement?
choices: Combining hydrogen and oxygen to make water in a fuel cell.
Heating limestone so calcium carbonate breaks apart.
Mixing two ionic solutions so a precipitate forms.
Using chlorine to replace bromide ions in solution.
questions.explanation: Chlorine can replace bromide, producing bromine.

questions.prompt: What do welding railroad tracks and joining electrical conductors have in common in this topic?
choices: They mainly use pressure to force solid metals together without melting.
They can use energy-releasing single replacement reactions to produce molten metal.
They both use double replacement reactions to make a solid precipitate.
They both use decomposition reactions to split metal oxides into gases.
questions.explanation: Both applications use replacement reactions that generate useful heat and metal products.

questions.prompt: What happens in a double replacement reaction?
choices: One element replaces a metal ion in one compound.
One compound absorbs energy and separates into simpler substances.
Positive ions of two ionic compounds exchange partners to form two new compounds.
A hydrocarbon reacts with oxygen to form carbon dioxide and water.
questions.explanation: Double replacement involves ion exchange between two ionic compounds.

questions.prompt: Which pattern represents double replacement?
choices: \(AX + BY -> AY + BX\)
\(A + BX -> AX + B\)
questions.explanation: The positive ions A and B exchange negative-ion partners.

questions.prompt: Which equation is a double replacement reaction?
choices: \(Zn + CuCl_2 -> ZnCl_2 + Cu\)
\(BaCl_2 + Na_2SO_4 -> BaSO_4 + 2NaCl\)
questions.explanation: Barium and sodium exchange ion partners, forming two new ionic compounds.

questions.prompt: What is a precipitate?
choices: A gas product that escapes from an aqueous mixture.
A solid that forms from a reaction between solutions.
A dissolved ion that remains aqueous after mixing.
A product identified by a coefficient rather than a state symbol.
questions.explanation: A precipitate is an insoluble solid product.

questions.prompt: Two clear ionic solutions are mixed and a cloudy yellow solid appears. What type of evidence is this?
choices: Evidence that a gas-forming reaction occurred.
Evidence that the ions stayed dissolved without rearranging.
Precipitate formation, often linked to a double replacement reaction.
A sign that dissolved ions stopped moving before reacting.
questions.explanation: A new solid can indicate ions exchanged partners and formed an insoluble product.

questions.prompt: In \(Pb(NO_3)_2(aq) + 2KI(aq) -> PbI_2(s) + 2KNO_3(aq)\), which product is the precipitate?
choices: \(KI(aq)\)
questions.explanation: The state symbol (s) identifies solid lead(II) iodide as the precipitate.

questions.prompt: What is a combustion reaction?
choices: A reaction where one compound absorbs energy and breaks into simpler products.
A reaction where ions exchange partners in aqueous solution.
A reaction where oxygen is produced as one of the products.
A reaction in which a substance reacts with oxygen and releases energy, often forming oxides.
questions.explanation: Combustion commonly involves oxygen and releases heat, often light.

questions.prompt: Which equation shows combustion of sulfur?
choices: \(2Na + Cl_2 -> 2NaCl\)
\(AgNO_3 + NaCl -> AgCl + NaNO_3\)
questions.explanation: Sulfur reacts with oxygen to form sulfur dioxide, an oxide.

questions.prompt: Which clue most strongly suggests a reaction is combustion?
choices: One compound splits into simpler products after absorbing energy.
Two aqueous solutions form a solid precipitate.
Oxygen is a reactant and heat or light is released.
A metal replaces another metal ion.
questions.explanation: Combustion is associated with oxygen and energy release.

questions.prompt: What elements are found in hydrocarbons?
choices: Carbon and hydrogen
Carbon and oxygen
Hydrogen and oxygen
Carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen
questions.explanation: Hydrocarbons are compounds containing carbon and hydrogen.

questions.prompt: What are the products of complete hydrocarbon combustion?
choices: Carbon monoxide and soot.
Hydrogen gas and oxygen gas.
A metal oxide and a salt.
Carbon dioxide and water.
questions.explanation: Complete combustion of hydrocarbons produces CO2 and H2O.

questions.prompt: Which fuel example is a hydrocarbon in this topic?
choices: Carbon dioxide, \(CO_2\)
Propane, \(C_3H_8\)
Water, \(H_2O\)
Sulfur dioxide, \(SO_2\)
questions.explanation: Propane contains carbon and hydrogen.

questions.prompt: Which general pattern represents complete hydrocarbon combustion?
choices: \(AX + BY -> AY + BX\)
\(A + BX -> AX + B\)
questions.explanation: Hydrocarbons react with oxygen to form carbon dioxide and water.

questions.prompt: Which balanced equation matches complete methane combustion?
questions.explanation: The balanced equation conserves 1 C, 4 H, and 4 O atoms.

questions.prompt: For propane combustion, \(C_3H_8 + 5O_2 -> 3CO_2 + 4H_2O\), how many water molecules are produced per propane molecule?
questions.explanation: The coefficient 4 in front of H2O gives four water molecules.

kp-280.title: KP4: Combustion as an exothermic process

questions.prompt: Why is combustion usually described as exothermic?
choices: It uses oxygen, so energy must be absorbed from the surroundings overall.
It releases energy to the surroundings, often as heat and light.
It forms oxides, so bond changes do not affect energy transfer.
It is classified by oxygen use, while energy transfer is a separate reaction type.
questions.explanation: Most combustion reactions release useful heat and often light.

questions.prompt: Which observation is evidence that a propane torch reaction is exothermic?
choices: The products include water vapour.
The equation has oxygen as a reactant.
A hot flame is produced.
The fuel is stored in a tank.
questions.explanation: A flame and heat show energy being released to the surroundings.

questions.prompt: A camp stove burns methane and warms a pot of water. Where does the useful heating energy come from?
choices: Energy released during methane combustion is transferred to the surroundings, including the pot and water.
The pot stores energy first and then transfers it into the flame.
The water decomposes into hydrogen fuel before the stove heats it.
Oxygen absorbs the energy so less reaches the pot.
questions.explanation: Combustion releases energy that can be transferred for cooking or heating.

questions.prompt: A fuel contains a small amount of sulfur. Which outcome best explains why burning it can contribute to air pollution?
choices: The sulfur atoms mainly stay in the fuel residue, so oxide gases are minor.
The fuel still reacts with oxygen, but sulfur is treated as a spectator element.
Sulfur can react with oxygen to form sulfur dioxide, an oxide pollutant.
Sulfur changes the flame colour but does not affect the combustion products.
questions.explanation: Elements or compounds containing sulfur can form sulfur dioxide during combustion.

questions.prompt: Why can gases such as sulfur dioxide and nitrogen dioxide be linked to acid precipitation?
choices: They can react in the atmosphere and help form acidic compounds.
They are already acids, so no further atmospheric reaction is involved.
They change cloud temperature, which lowers pH without forming acids.
They settle out before reacting with water in the atmosphere.
questions.explanation: These oxide pollutants can participate in reactions that produce acids in precipitation.

questions.prompt: Which formula best represents the oxide commonly formed when sulfur burns in oxygen?
questions.explanation: Sulfur dioxide, \(SO_2\), is a sulfur oxide formed in this combustion reaction.

questions.prompt: A Bunsen burner flame becomes yellow and smoky after the air intake is partly closed. What is the best explanation?
choices: The fuel is reacting with extra oxygen, so more soot forms.
There is too little oxygen, so incomplete combustion is more likely.
The burner is producing mostly carbon dioxide and water vapour.
The flame is producing a neutralization reaction instead of combustion.
questions.explanation: Limited oxygen can keep the fuel from burning completely.

questions.prompt: Which set of products is most consistent with incomplete combustion of a hydrocarbon?
choices: Mostly carbon dioxide and water, because the oxygen supply is enough.
Hydrogen gas and oxygen gas, because the hydrocarbon separates into elements.
Carbon monoxide and carbon particles, along with some carbon dioxide and water.
A salt and water, because the hydrocarbon acts like an acid.
questions.explanation: Incomplete combustion can produce CO and carbon particles because fuel is not fully oxidized.

questions.prompt: Why is soot evidence that combustion has not gone fully to completion?
choices: Some carbon from the fuel remains as solid particles instead of becoming carbon dioxide.
Soot forms when extra oxygen turns carbon dioxide back into carbon.
Soot indicates the fuel's carbon was converted completely into carbon dioxide.
Soot is the salt produced by acid-base neutralization.
questions.explanation: Soot is a sign that carbon from the fuel was not fully converted to carbon dioxide.

questions.prompt: Why can carbon monoxide build-up be dangerous before people notice a problem?
choices: It is sometimes assumed to be visible when soot is present.
It has a smell similar to smoke, so people may confuse it with ordinary exhaust.
It is mainly a pH hazard rather than a breathing hazard.
It is colourless, odourless, and toxic.
questions.explanation: People may not detect carbon monoxide without a detector.

questions.prompt: Which situation has the greatest carbon monoxide risk?
choices: Lighting a candle briefly in a well-ventilated room.
Running a fuel-burning engine in a closed garage.
Testing a neutral salt solution with universal indicator.
Letting carbonated water sit open on a lab bench.
questions.explanation: Poorly ventilated fuel combustion can produce and trap carbon monoxide.

questions.prompt: A home has a gas furnace and a fireplace. What is the main role of a carbon monoxide detector?
choices: It adds oxygen to nearby flames so incomplete combustion stops.
It converts carbon monoxide into carbon dioxide before people breathe it.
It warns people when a hard-to-detect toxic gas may be present.
It measures pH changes caused by furnace gases.
