questions.explanation: Detectors provide warning when human senses may not.

questions.prompt: In this chemistry unit, what makes a dissolved substance an acid?
choices: It releases \(H^+\) ions into the aqueous solution.
It releases \(OH^-\) ions as the main ion in solution.
It contains hydrogen in its formula, even if it does not ionize in water.
It must have a pH value greater than 7.
questions.explanation: Acids increase hydrogen ion concentration in water.

questions.prompt: Which formula is most likely to represent an acid in water?
choices: \(NaOH(aq)\)
\(KOH(aq)\)
\(NaCl(aq)\)
questions.explanation: \(HNO_3(aq)\) is an acid because it can produce hydrogen ions in solution.

questions.prompt: An unknown colourless solution turns blue litmus red. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: The solution is probably neutral because it has no visible colour.
The solution is acidic.
The solution is basic because litmus changed colour.
The solution is neutral because indicators react with any colourless liquid.
questions.explanation: Acids turn blue litmus red and have pH values below 7.

questions.prompt: What defines a base in aqueous solution?
choices: It releases \(H^+\) ions into water.
It contains hydrogen and oxygen somewhere in the formula.
It forms \(OH^-\) ions when dissolved in water.
It is any solution that feels slippery before testing.
questions.explanation: Bases increase hydroxide ion concentration in water.

questions.prompt: Which formula is most likely to represent a base in water?
choices: \(HCl(aq)\)
\(CH_3COOH(aq)\)
questions.explanation: \(Ca(OH)_2\) contains hydroxide ions and can form a basic solution.

questions.prompt: A cleaner has pH 11 and turns red litmus blue. What does this indicate?
choices: It is acidic because any pH number above 10 is very acidic.
It is neutral because cleaners are usually diluted with water.
It is acidic because it changes litmus colour strongly.
It is basic because pH is above 7 and red litmus turns blue.
questions.explanation: Bases turn red litmus blue and have pH values above 7.

questions.prompt: What information does an acid-base indicator provide?
choices: It identifies the exact formula of the dissolved compound.
It changes colour in response to acidic or basic conditions.
It changes the solution by reacting with the acid or base.
It measures the mass of \(H^+\) ions directly.
questions.explanation: Indicators respond to hydrogen ion concentration or pH range.

questions.prompt: Blue litmus paper turns red in an unknown solution. What is the best interpretation?
choices: The solution is basic because red means high pH.
The solution is neutral because litmus changed colour.
The solution is acidic because acids turn blue litmus red.
The solution is acidic because bases turn blue litmus red.
questions.explanation: Blue litmus turning red is evidence of acidity.

questions.prompt: Why might a pH meter or universal indicator be chosen instead of simple litmus paper?
choices: They can give a pH value or narrower pH range rather than broad acid/base evidence.
They remove the hydrogen ions from the solution during testing.
They automatically balance the acid-base equation.
They identify the salt that will form during neutralization.
questions.explanation: Litmus mostly classifies acid/base, while other tools can provide pH information.

questions.prompt: Two samples have pH 3 and pH 5. Which statement is correct?
choices: pH 5 is more acidic because it is closer to neutral.
Both samples have the same acidity because they are both below 7.
pH 5 has more \(H^+\) because the pH number is larger.
pH 3 is more acidic because lower pH means stronger acidity.
questions.explanation: Acidic solutions have pH values below 7, and lower pH means more acidic.

questions.prompt: Which pH value would be classified as basic?
choices: pH 6
pH 11
pH 7
pH 3
questions.explanation: Basic solutions have pH values above 7.

questions.prompt: What does pH 7 usually indicate for pure water?
choices: It is weakly acidic because water contains hydrogen atoms.
It is weakly basic because 7 is greater than most acidic values.
It is neutral, with acidity and basicity balanced.
It is acidic because it contains hydrogen atoms.
questions.explanation: Neutral solutions have pH equal to 7.

questions.prompt: In an acidic aqueous solution, how do \(H^+\) and \(OH^-\) compare?
choices: \(H^+\) concentration is greater than \(OH^-\) concentration.
\(OH^-\) concentration is greater than \(H^+\) concentration.
The two ions are equal because acidic solutions still contain water.
Aqueous acids contain hydroxide ions but not hydrogen ions.
questions.explanation: Acids have more hydrogen ions than hydroxide ions.

questions.prompt: A solution changes from pH 5 to pH 4. What happens to the hydrogen ion concentration?
choices: It decreases because a smaller pH number means fewer ions.
It stays nearly the same because both values are acidic.
It becomes half as large.
It increases by about 10 times.
questions.explanation: Each one-unit drop in pH means about 10 times more hydrogen ions.

questions.prompt: A solution has pH 12. Which statement best describes its ion balance?
choices: \(H^+\) and \(OH^-\) are equal because pH values near the middle balance.
\(OH^-\) is more abundant than \(H^+\).
\(H^+\) is more abundant than \(OH^-\).
The pH value describes indicator colour rather than ion balance.
questions.explanation: pH greater than 7 indicates a basic solution with more hydroxide ions.

questions.prompt: What are the usual products when an acid reacts with a base in a neutralization reaction?
choices: A hydrocarbon and oxygen
Carbon dioxide and water, as in combustion
A salt and water
A metal and a separate non-metal
questions.explanation: An acid and base react to form water and an ionic compound called a salt.

questions.prompt: Which situation best represents neutralization in everyday life?
choices: A basic antacid reacts with excess stomach acid.
A fuel burns in oxygen and releases heat.
Salt crystals dissolve in water without reacting with an acid.
Iron combines with oxygen to form an oxide.
questions.explanation: Antacids contain bases that react with acid.

questions.prompt: During neutralization, why does the pH often move closer to 7?
choices: \(H^+\) ions move into the salt while \(OH^-\) ions stay dissolved.
\(OH^-\) ions become oxygen gas before the solution can approach neutral.
The salt controls pH by absorbing the indicator from the solution.
\(H^+\) and \(OH^-\) combine to form water.
questions.explanation: Hydrogen ions and hydroxide ions form water molecules.

questions.prompt: Which symbolic pattern best represents a neutralization reaction?
choices: \(AB + C \rightarrow CB + A\)
\(HA + BOH \rightarrow BA + HOH\)
\(C_xH_y + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2O\)
\(AB \rightarrow A + B\)
questions.explanation: Neutralization is an acid-base reaction that forms salt and water.

questions.prompt: What are the products of \(HCl + KOH\) in a neutralization reaction?
choices: \(HCl + KOH\) left unchanged because both dissolve in water
\(KCl + H_2O\)
questions.explanation: The hydrogen ion and hydroxide ion form water, while potassium and chloride form potassium chloride.

questions.prompt: Why is neutralization classified as a double replacement reaction?
choices: The ions exchange partners: \(H^+\) joins \(OH^-\), and the remaining ions form a salt.
A single compound breaks into its elements.
One metal atom replaces hydrogen in an acid.
A hydrocarbon reacts with oxygen to form oxides.
questions.explanation: Neutralization follows the double replacement pattern because ions switch partners to form water and a salt.

kp-291.title: KP1: Fossil fuels and carbon dioxide

questions.prompt: Why does burning a fossil fuel usually add \(CO_2\) to the atmosphere?
choices: The oxygen in air supplies the carbon atoms found in \(CO_2\).
The fuel absorbs \(CO_2\) during burning and releases it afterward.
Carbon atoms in the fuel combine with oxygen during combustion.
The reaction changes carbon products into neutral salt crystals.
questions.explanation: Fossil fuels contain carbon, and complete combustion produces carbon dioxide.

questions.prompt: Why is carbon dioxide discussed in both climate and ocean chemistry?
choices: It is a greenhouse gas and can dissolve in ocean water.
It is a strong base with pH 14.
It forms carbon monoxide before affecting ocean chemistry.
It affects climate, but ocean water keeps it from dissolving.
questions.explanation: CO2 affects the atmosphere and can change ocean chemistry.

questions.prompt: Which process is most directly linked to fossil-fuel \(CO_2\) emissions?
choices: Neutralizing a basic cleaner with lemon juice
Dissolving table salt in ocean water
Filtering a precipitate from an ionic solution
Combustion of carbon-containing fuels
questions.explanation: Burning hydrocarbons produces carbon dioxide and water.

kp-292.title: KP2: Carbon dioxide and ocean acidity

questions.prompt: What can happen after atmospheric \(CO_2\) dissolves in ocean water?
choices: It immediately forms hydroxide ions and raises ocean pH.
It can form carbonic acid, which can increase \(H^+\) in the water.
It reacts with dissolved salts to make oxygen gas and neutral water.
It removes acids from seawater by changing them into carbonate ions.
questions.explanation: CO2 and water can form carbonic acid, changing ocean chemistry.

questions.prompt: If more dissolved \(CO_2\) leads to more \(H^+\) in seawater, what happens to pH?
choices: pH rises because more \(H^+\) means the water is becoming more basic.
pH stays fixed at 7 because seawater contains many dissolved substances.
pH decreases because the water becomes more acidic.
pH becomes a coefficient in the reaction equation.
questions.explanation: Increasing hydrogen ion concentration lowers pH and increases acidity.

questions.prompt: Why can extra dissolved \(CO_2\) reduce carbonate ion availability?
choices: Some \(H^+\) combines with \(CO_3^{2-}\), forming bicarbonate ions.
Carbonate ions change into carbon dioxide gas without using hydrogen ions.
Carbonate ions become calcium ions when water becomes more acidic.
\(CO_2\) prevents hydrogen ions from existing in seawater.
questions.explanation: Hydrogen ions can combine with carbonate ions, leaving fewer carbonate ions available.

questions.prompt: Why can ocean acidification make shell-building harder for some organisms?
choices: It increases carbonate ions, so organisms spend energy removing extras.
It changes calcium carbonate shells into carbon dioxide fuel.
It makes shell material form more slowly because calcium becomes unavailable.
It can reduce carbonate ions needed to make calcium carbonate shells.
questions.explanation: Many shells and skeletons depend on carbonate materials.

questions.prompt: Which group is most directly connected to carbonate-based shells or skeletons?
choices: Combustion fuels such as gasoline and propane
Clams, oysters, some plankton, and corals
Land plants whose stems contain cellulose
Copper wire heated in a combustion test
questions.explanation: The textbook describes shelled organisms, plankton, and coral reefs as vulnerable.

questions.prompt: Why can a decrease in shell-building plankton affect larger ocean food webs?
choices: Plankton changes stay local because larger organisms do not depend on them.
Food webs are affected mainly by predators, so plankton are less important.
Some plankton form the base of food chains that support other organisms.
Acidic water mostly changes rock colour before affecting organisms.
questions.explanation: Changes to organisms near the base of food chains can affect many other organisms.

questions.prompt: Why is ocean acidification both a chemistry issue and a social issue?
choices: Chemical changes can affect ecosystems, food supply, and people's livelihoods.
Chemistry issues are mainly about equations, while social effects come from politics.
Ocean pH changes are useful for chemistry but separate from food supply.
Social impacts can be evaluated without chemical or ecological evidence.
questions.explanation: Chemical changes can have ecological and human consequences.

questions.prompt: Which evidence would best support an evaluation of ocean acidification?
choices: A map showing ocean regions without chemistry or organism data
A guess about whether seawater would taste sour
The number of carbon and oxygen atoms in one \(CO_2\) molecule
pH data, carbonate chemistry, organism health, and effects on communities
questions.explanation: A strong evaluation connects chemical evidence with ecosystem and human impacts.

questions.prompt: Which question best connects ocean chemistry to human consequences?
choices: How many chemical symbols appear in the ocean-acidification equation?
How might lower carbonate availability affect shellfish harvests?
Should pH data be ignored because ions are too small to see?
Do ocean organisms respond in similar ways regardless of shell material?
questions.explanation: Shellfish harvests connect chemical changes to food supply and livelihoods.

kp-295.title: KP1: Properties of acids and bases

questions.prompt: Which evidence pattern best supports the claim that an unknown is acidic?
choices: pH above 7 and red litmus turns blue
pH near 7 and neither litmus paper changes
pH below 7 and blue litmus turns red
A smoky yellow flame and soot form during heating
questions.explanation: Acids have pH below 7 and turn blue litmus red.

questions.prompt: Which set of results best identifies a base?
choices: pH above 7 and red litmus turns blue
pH below 7 and blue litmus turns red
pH near 7 with little indicator change
Carbon monoxide production
questions.explanation: Bases have pH above 7 and turn red litmus blue.

questions.prompt: Why might a student use more than one acid-base test on an unknown?
choices: Extra tests remove the need for goggles and careful disposal.
One indicator gives enough evidence to identify the exact compound formula.
Indicators provide colour, but the colour should not be connected to pH.
Several observations can narrow the pH range and make the claim stronger.
questions.explanation: Using several tests can support a stronger acid-base classification.

questions.prompt: What is the safest first step before testing several unknown acid-base solutions?
choices: Smell each sample closely so the procedure can be shorter.
Plan a teacher-approved procedure and put on required PPE.
Mix the unknowns together after recording their labels.
Heat each sample before deciding which tests are needed.
questions.explanation: Unknown solutions require careful planning, PPE, and teacher-approved procedures.

questions.prompt: Why should each spot plate well or test tube be labelled during unknown-solution testing?
choices: Labels change the pH so indicators can be compared fairly.
Labels reduce chemical hazards by neutralizing spills.
Labels let observations be matched to the correct sample.
Labels replace the need to record observations.
questions.explanation: Good lab organization prevents mixing up evidence.

questions.prompt: Which planned test could help classify an unknown as acidic, basic, or neutral?
choices: Testing with litmus, pH paper, or another acid-base indicator
Checking whether the sample container feels warm from handling
Balancing a combustion equation for a different substance
Measuring the outside length of the test tube
questions.explanation: Indicators and pH tests provide acid-base evidence.

questions.prompt: An unknown turns blue litmus red and has a pH near 3. What claim is best supported?
choices: The unknown is neutral because pH values are measured on the same scale.
The unknown is basic because an indicator changed colour.
The unknown is a hydrocarbon fuel.
The unknown is acidic.
questions.explanation: Both observations support an acidic classification.

questions.prompt: Why might an investigation report a pH range instead of one exact pH value?
choices: A pH range means the student forgot to record observations.
Some indicators change colour over a range rather than at one exact pH.
A pH range suggests the unknown contains no ions.
Indicators give precise pH values when the colour is recorded carefully.
questions.explanation: Indicator colour changes often correspond to a range of possible pH values.

questions.prompt: Which explanation is evidence-based?
choices: Unknown B is basic because its label looked like a household cleaner.
Unknown B is basic because it looked similar to another sample.
Unknown B is basic because it had pH 10 and turned red litmus blue.
Unknown B is basic because it was tested last.
questions.explanation: The correct explanation uses recorded test results.

questions.prompt: Which reaction type has the pattern \(AB -> A + B\)?
choices: Decomposition
Synthesis
Double replacement
Neutralization
questions.explanation: A compound breaking apart is decomposition.

questions.prompt: A reaction mixes two ionic solutions and forms an insoluble solid. Which type is most likely?
choices: Combustion
Synthesis
Decomposition
Double replacement
questions.explanation: Double replacement reactions often involve ions exchanging partners and can form precipitates.

questions.prompt: Which reaction is best classified as combustion?
choices: \(2Na + Cl_2 -> 2NaCl\)
\(AgNO_3 + KCl -> AgCl + KNO_3\)
questions.explanation: A hydrocarbon reacting with oxygen to form carbon dioxide and water is combustion.

questions.prompt: Predict the products for complete combustion of \(C_4H_{10}\).
choices: \(CO\) and \(H_2O\)
questions.explanation: Complete hydrocarbon combustion produces carbon dioxide and water.

questions.prompt: Predict the main product when magnesium reacts with chlorine gas.
choices: \(MgCl_2\)
\(Mg_2Cl\)
\(MgCl\)
questions.explanation: A metal and non-metal combine to form an ionic compound, magnesium chloride.

questions.prompt: For \(HBr + Ca(OH)_2\), which products should be predicted before balancing?
choices: \(CaO + HBr\)
\(CaH_2 + BrOH\)
\(CaBr_2 + H_2O\)
questions.explanation: Acid-base neutralization forms a salt, calcium bromide, and water.

questions.prompt: Which balanced equation represents potassium reacting with chlorine gas?
choices: \(K + Cl_2 -> KCl\)
\(K_2 + Cl -> KCl\)
questions.explanation: The balanced equation has 2 potassium atoms and 2 chlorine atoms on both sides.

questions.prompt: Which equation correctly balances the complete combustion of ethane, \(C_2H_6\)?
questions.explanation: The balanced equation conserves 4 carbon atoms, 12 hydrogen atoms, and 14 oxygen atoms.

questions.prompt: After predicting products, what must still be done before an equation is complete?
choices: Balance the equation using coefficients so atoms are conserved.
Change subscripts until the equation looks shorter.
Choose products with smaller formulas if coefficients become large.
Check the reaction type but leave atom counts for the final report.
questions.explanation: Conservation of mass requires balanced atom counts.

questions.prompt: A student mixes two clear solutions and a cloudy solid appears while the temperature stays nearly the same. Which observation is most useful for classifying the reaction?
choices: The lack of temperature change means energy evidence is weak, but other evidence still matters.
A cloudy solid suggests a precipitate rather than a trapped combustion gas.
The formation of a precipitate suggests a double replacement reaction may have occurred.
The reaction must be neutralization because both reactants were liquids.
questions.explanation: A new insoluble solid is evidence of a chemical change and often supports double replacement reasoning.

questions.prompt: In a lab, a candle burns and a cool beaker held above it becomes foggy inside. Which classification is best supported?
choices: Combustion, because a fuel reacted with oxygen and produced water vapour among the products.
Decomposition, because the beaker broke the wax into elements.
Single replacement, because oxygen replaced carbon in the wax.
Neutralization, because the flame became smaller.
questions.explanation: Burning candle wax is a combustion reaction; water vapour on the beaker is one clue that hydrogen in the fuel formed water.

questions.prompt: Why should observations be connected to reactants and products before deciding a reaction type?
choices: Visible evidence should be listed after the reaction type has already been chosen.
Reaction type depends mainly on whether the temperature changed.
Equations are most useful for reactions that produce light or gas.
Different reaction types can share visible signs, so the chemical pattern must also be considered.
questions.explanation: Evidence such as bubbles, precipitates, heat, or colour change is helpful, but classification also depends on the pattern of reactants and products.

questions.prompt: Why can an antacid reduce the effect of excess stomach acid?
choices: It dilutes stomach acid without changing the ions involved.
It contains a basic substance that reacts with acid to form less acidic products.
It turns stomach acid into a fuel-like gas.
It works mainly by cooling the stomach acid.
questions.explanation: Antacids are bases or basic compounds that neutralize acid, producing products such as a salt and water, and sometimes carbon dioxide.

questions.prompt: A tablet containing magnesium hydroxide reacts with hydrochloric acid. Which equation is balanced?
choices: \(Mg(OH)_2 + HCl -> MgCl + H_2O\)
\(Mg(OH)_2 + 2HCl -> MgH_2 + 2ClOH\)
\(Mg(OH)_2 + 2HCl -> MgCl_2 + 2H_2O\)
questions.explanation: Two hydrochloric acid units are needed to neutralize the two hydroxide groups, forming magnesium chloride and water.

questions.prompt: A student says, "The best antacid is the one that makes the solution basic." What is the best correction?
choices: An antacid should neutralize excess acid, not necessarily make the final solution strongly basic.
A strong base is safest if it changes pH quickly.
Neutralization means the solution must become pH 14.
Antacids work by adding more acid.
questions.explanation: The goal is to reduce acidity toward a safer range; making the solution strongly basic could create a different problem.

questions.prompt: A group compares three antacid brands by adding each to the same volume and concentration of acid. What is the independent variable?
choices: The final pH measurement.
The volume and concentration of acid kept the same.
The amount of acid controlled in each trial.
The antacid brand being tested.
questions.explanation: The independent variable is what the group changes between trials: the antacid being compared.

questions.prompt: Which setup is the fairest way to compare how much acid different antacids can neutralize?
choices: Use a different acid concentration for each brand to save time.
Use the same acid volume and concentration, same tablet mass, and same endpoint method for each antacid.
Crush one tablet but leave the others whole, then compare how quickly each trial changes pH.
Stop each trial at different pH values based on how quickly each brand reacts.
questions.explanation: Controlled conditions help ensure that differences in results are caused by the antacid, not by inconsistent testing.

questions.prompt: In an antacid investigation, final pH is measured after each trial. What role does final pH play?
choices: It is an independent variable because the group chooses it before testing.
It is a controlled variable because it must start different each time.
It is a dependent variable because it is measured as a result of the test.
It is unrelated to neutralization.
questions.explanation: The dependent variable is what is measured to judge the effect of the independent variable.

questions.prompt: Two antacids both neutralize the same amount of acid. Antacid A costs less per dose, but Antacid B needs a smaller mass. What is the best conclusion?
choices: The recommendation depends on which effectiveness criteria are most important.
Antacid A is best if cost is listed before the chemistry results.
Antacid B is best if a smaller mass seems easier to swallow.
Neither antacid worked because the results were not identical.
questions.explanation: Effectiveness can include neutralizing ability, amount needed, cost, side effects, and other evidence-based criteria.

questions.prompt: A class decides that the best antacid should neutralize acid with the fewest side effects and a reasonable cost. Which evidence best supports a recommendation?
choices: A single student's favourite package colour.
The tablet that fizzed the loudest, with no other data.
The antacid with the longest brand name.
A data table comparing neutralizing ability, dose needed, cost, and side-effect notes for each antacid.
questions.explanation: A recommendation should match the chosen criteria and use multiple pieces of evidence.

questions.prompt: Why can choosing an antacid from only one measurement be misleading?
choices: Scientific decisions should use the easiest measurement and ignore harder criteria.
One measurement may ignore other important criteria such as cost, dose, safety, or side effects.
A single pH change is enough to prove cost, dose, and side-effect suitability.
Neutralization is easier to judge from package claims than from lab data.
questions.explanation: The textbook-style investigation asks students to decide which properties matter, so the conclusion should be based on those criteria together.

questions.prompt: Which balanced equation represents hydrochloric acid reacting with sodium hydroxide?
choices: \(HCl + NaOH -> NaH + ClOH\)
\(2HCl + NaOH -> NaCl_2 + H_2O\)
\(HCl + NaOH -> NaCl + H_2O\)
\(HCl + NaOH -> NaCl + H_2\)
questions.explanation: A simple acid-base neutralization forms a salt, sodium chloride, and water with atoms conserved.

questions.prompt: Calcium carbonate can act as an antacid. Which balanced equation fits its reaction with hydrochloric acid?
choices: \(CaCO_3 + 2HCl -> CaCl_2 + CO_2 + H_2O\)
\(CaCO_3 + HCl -> CaCl + CO_2 + H_2O\)
\(CaCO_3 + 2HCl -> CaH_2 + CCl_2 + O_3\)
\(CaCO_3 -> CaO + CO_2 + HCl\)
questions.explanation: Carbonate antacids react with acid to form a salt, carbon dioxide, and water; two HCl units balance the chloride and hydrogen atoms.

questions.prompt: When writing a balanced neutralization equation for an antacid, what should be checked last?
choices: That subscripts have been changed until the equation looks balanced.
That the acid formula is copied unchanged before coefficients are added.
That water is treated as optional if it makes balancing harder.
That each element has the same number of atoms on both sides of the equation.
questions.explanation: Balancing uses coefficients to conserve atoms; changing subscripts would change the substances.
